posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked: I read two reports that 2020 vintage will be high quality and prices might be similar to 2019 or fall further. This is what I thought might happen and didnt go overboard with my 2019 purchases. Let’s discuss!

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
I’m expecting prices to come in line somewhere between 18/19. Wines that discounted heavily sold out on the morning, and moved quickly on the secondary market. I think some prices will be lower than 19 but that’ll be mostly the wines that didnt discount heavily.

Remember, Latour 2013 had a chance to set a precedent by lowering their price against 2012 Latour and they chose not to - instead choosing to release in line.

I think O(15%) off 2018 pricing is probably about where most wines will come out, so 5-15% above 2019.


Bear in mind the way the system is architected, most of the chateau wont really feel the impact themselves anyway. Furthermore, Rhone 2019 releases were in line with historic pricing. Champagne mostly was as well? I’m no Burgundy or Italian expert but I didnt get any impression of decreased pricing there either, so why would Bordeaux single handedly buck the trend?


I hope I’m wrong, but I’ve learned to be cynical over the last few years



The problem with 2020 prices coming out lower than 19 is that you undermine the historic market, and suddenly all back wine looks ‘expensive’. Decades of gains for the chateau will be eroded, and bear in mind many chateau are now ‘professionally’ owned, investors need to get their returns.


vested interest: I went pretty much as big as I could on 19, but had a sizeable 18 position as well.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked: Heard from people on the ground that 2020 is yet another very good vintage - probably not on the level of 2019 but still very good. Pricing for sure will not be on par with 2019 but higher. Yes, the hospitality industry is still in dire straits but the fine wine buying private customers are better of than a year and two years ago. I hear record demand here in central Europe from private clients for the most recent releases over the past few months.

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked: Personally I’m hopeful quality will be high in 2020 and prices affordable, just so I can have something positive come out of that horrific year. If so, I intend to be a buyer … then open these over the decades and reflect upon a lost year.

posts.name: Al3ks V3y
posts.cooked: It’s my sons birth year, so I’m certainly a buyer and will buy at least 1-2 first growths and a number of other classified growths / high end Pomerols. That said, I’ll still be pretty price sensitive, if numbers look like 2019 I’ll probably go hog wild on the Palmer, LMHB and VCC, otherwise will be more conservative. I though Palmer at $210 and $250 LMHB were screaming deals (and made my credit cards scream for mercy).

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
First off, nobody knows what will happen. But if you want a guess, 2020 will be more expensive than 2019.

Yields are lower in 2020, so less wine. Plus, the devastating frosts this week will reduce quantity for 2021. And the markets are up from last year, so buyers have more disposable income. Most importantly, the quality is said to be good all over the region. Starting Wednesday, I will have some idea of that as I begin tasting about 800 wines, assuming everything starts arriving on-time.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: If indeed 2020 is an excellent to great vintage, then there will be buyers. But I expect they’re be some resistance, as even with a small vintage, there is plenty of great wines from other vintages still waiting to get out of the negotiants’ cellars. Bordeaux has shown it can digest two to three excellent vintages, and apart from 2017, a vintage that I like and am backfilling, there are some really great years from 2014, and not a clunker in sight. Can they absorb six vintages? Not sure.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Thanks Jeff, that’s really insightful. I wasnt aware of the frost, and yes, that would definitely be an argument in terms of driving prices up. Is my lay understanding right that the negociant system partially insulates the chateau from end consumer pricing, as well? As they effectively can ‘mandate’ allocations to the negociants?

@Mark - agreed. There are a lot of back vintages still to be cleared out, but that’s never stopped them before really. Perosnally I’ve been picking up some very good QPR 2019s which werent bought out on primary release. 18 bottles of Brainaire-Ducru my most recent.

@Aleks - I personally (only amateur view, no itb expertise) wouldnt expect LMHB and Palmer to come out comparable to last year’s price. They sold out QUICKLY. Those are wines, imho, prime for pricing up. Palmer I only got 6 bottles from pre-purchasing, and I got 3 LMHB through BBR because someone turned down an allocation and BBR could give me 3 HB 3 LMHB.

posts.name: Paul Mc_C_ourt
posts.cooked: I expect prices to pop upwards fairly solidly.

2019 was released in the teeth of the COVID related severe global recession and US tariffs. 2020 is being released with an (uneven) global economic recovery and US tariffs off the table for now. Given a substantially better economy, less uncertainty, and poor yields across most of Bordeaux, it would be surprising if 2020 was released at 2019 or better prices.

Canon, Pontet, Chevalier, Berliquet (all of which I bought) are available at the same price now as they were EP. I think Rauzan Segla did move a bit and possibly Larcis Ducasse too

Have any of these wines been delivered yet…? I think it’s very possible that second tranche and later pricing for 2018 is similar to the initial first tranche price…but I would expect retail pricing to go up quite a bit especially if these are scored at the top band of their EP scores once reviewed from bottle. K&L is showing more Domaine de Chevalier pre-arrivals of several vintages back in stock (including 2009/2010/2016/2018). The pricing of the 2018 at $90 looks like a modest increase from the first tranche pricing. (15%+)

I would think that between 2016/18/19/20 the Bordelaise will have a lot of wine to sell, and it wouldn’t be surprising to see an increase on 2019 pricing, but below or in-line with 2018 in order to keep interest in 2020.

My experience of Bordeaux is that it is tough for them to sell two great back to back vintages even if the surrounding years are mediocre. The market is much larger than it used to be, but there is no way that they will able to sell the five out of six great vintages since 2015. And frankly I would even add 2014, which despite less love from the critics, I think will prove to be rather fine. Their marketing is better than it used to be, the demand is greater, but it is not at least double which it would need to be to absorb all that inventory.

Pickings are now slim, but they previously had Beychevelle and Clinet at ~$80, Tertre Roteboeuf at $140, etc. Those are at or near 2019 EP prices (at least 2nd tranche) and at a decent discount to other recent vintages.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
My experience of Bordeaux is that it is tough for them to sell two great back to back vintages even if the surrounding years are mediocre. The market is much larger than it used to be, but there is no way that they will able to sell the five out of six great vintages since 2015. And frankly I would even add 2014, which despite less love from the critics, I think will prove to be rather fine. Their marketing is better than it used to be, the demand is greater, but it is not at least double which it would need to be to absorb all that inventory.

while I agree with what you just said, with the negociant system, does it matter “as much”?

Low interest rates are allowing the negotiants to hold inventory. Even so, they can survive in the short term but not the long one. Gone are the days when negotiants really dictated to the chateaux; the pendulum has clearly shifted in favor of the (major) chateaux.

2020 does not sound like a vintage that will be as good as 2019, but if the pricing is close, they will sell some. If negotiants are forced to buy at higher prices, there will few sales. They will then they are stuck between a rock and a hard place. Accept the higher prices and the wines and hold ridiculous amounts of inventory, or refuse and lose the allocation.

A few factors will dictate the market. Low yields of 2021 may help but rising interest rates as economies cope with free spending from the cost of Covid, the Chinese buying less and large amounts of inventory still unsold are likely to cause some serious problems.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Here a few observations based on the few sources I checked so far (Jancis Robinson, James Suckling, JM Quarin and the Austrian wine magazine Falstaff):

a) It seems to be a right bank & Pessac year
b) overall quality is lower than in 16/18/19 (Jancis, Quarin are lower with less superstars; Suckling is always high) but still on a very good level above 2017
c) same elevated alcohol levels on the right bank as in 16/18/19
d) Pessac still has high alcohol levels with HB reaching crazy 15!
e) slightly lower alcohol levels in the rest of the left bank compared to 18/19, for some it’s even below 16 (but not all) - Lafite 12.8, Mouton 13.1

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: If its in line with 17, there’ll be basically no pressure to buy EP. 17 went down for the most part moving from EP to in bottle - I was burned on my small amount fo 17 purchases.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
There’s no fixed structure. 2018 was Larcis Ducasse & Palmer among the first IIRC, 2016 was Cos D’estournel, cant remember 17, 19 was obviously Pontet Canet.

It’s always a bit of a blinking game. There’s a risk that if you release early and dont sell out, other chateau might undercut you a bit, there’s also a risk that if you release early and sell out completely, everyone else raises their prices a bit to manage demand. If you release late, capital might be depleted and despite a great wine at a good price, not sell out…

Tough game!

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I told friend from whom I buy almost all my futures that I was only going to buy VCC this year. My cellar is saturated, there is way too much young wine available. Five out of the last six vintages have been worth buying selectively, so why would I need to buy futures? I am done.

Primeur is generally a mug’s game; in the last twenty years only 2008 and 2019 have made any sense financially, unless you buy odd sizes (I buy magnums and half bottles). 2020 is still going to generate some excitement; a good to great vintage always does. But I will happily bet that you will be able to buy the same wines ten years from now within 10% of the futures price.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
With a couple of caveats I will take that bet with the stakes being a bottle of 2015 Pichon Lalande.

  1. The main thing is deciding what the true opening price is. A few chateaux release a tiny part of their production at artificially low prices, as an incentive to the negotiants. None of this wine ever trickles through to the consumer. So the pricing is based on the realistic first price you can buy it for.

  2. The price ten years from now is the lowest price we can find, be it retail or auction.

  3. Let us agree on five wines BEFORE all the scores come out, you take three I take two. My two would be Issan and Pichon Baron.

Should be fun.

I do think you’re right in that, ultimately, d’Issan and Pichon Baron are wines that are unlikely to appreciate in the short term. No contest there. We’re also flying blind a bit because we dont know what the price of these wines will come out at, of course.




I’m happy to take the bet on those terms.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Can I ask a dumb question of Henry and Mark since I am relatively new to buying high end Bordeaux in quantity: How does one get allocations for wines like LaFleur and VCC / first tranche pricing? Is it simply buying a lot of wine from futures retailers and eventually getting invited to buy the highly allocated stuff?

I was able to get 1 bottle of VCC last year through Vinfolio and would probably have bit on lafleur if I could have found it easily enough for the right price. In 2018 VCC was a bit easier to find (MacArthur still had for sale) for what it’s worth.

I simply asked, to be honest. 2019 was a good year for getting wines typically on allocation, as restaurants were shut/had capital problems/etc. I’ll DM you something separately.

I dont know where you are from, but its also important to know who are the direct importers versus who is getting an allocation from an importer and selling it on.

Depends on what you mean by first tranche. Those tiny allocations I talked about earlier never actually make it into the marketplace. They are kept by the negotiants who then incorporate it into the next tranche and then average it out. The chateau can then point to the first price and say how cheap it was, it was quickly sold which allows them to raise prices.

This is far less prevalent than it used to be as the market is much more transparent. Most chateaux release decent amounts and there are no games involved.

Real opening prices tend to be trumpeted quite loudly, and you should get e mails from your favorite retailers. Make sure you are on their mailing list, and since there tend to be a ton of offers I would suggest creating a new e mail address for wine. Go with larger retailers such as Zachys, Wallys etc. I also like Millesima in NYC as they are owned by a negotiant.

As for Lafleur and VCC, I never seem to have a problem getting offers.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz

I bought 2010 Lafleur, for me the Right Bank wine of the vintage. If I were to sell my six pack now, I will lose a little money.

Buying futures is not really a financial certainty, in fact unless you are buying one of the below market vintages such as 2019, the likelihood is you will lose money. I buy them because

  1. I want to know that I have the best possible examples of the wine.
  2. There is little premium for magnums or half bottles, so I can get the formats I want and
    3.There is a fun part to buying in advance, getting the wine delivered and drinking it twenty years later. Almost a little like parenting.

I buy much more heavily in vintages like 2019, but I think it is interesting that a couple of weeks ago I was able to buy 2019 Palmer one of the steals of the vintage for the opening price.

2020 is going to be interesting. Nobody that has published to date has suggested that it is as good as some of the better recent vintages, and one or two damned it with faint praise calling it better than 2017. Personally I am not buying apart from VCC to complete the vertical, but that may be age related.

Wines like Gruaud Laroze , Talbot , d’Issan and yes Petit Villages get 95+ scores . I will wait for a few more reviews , including Jeff’s very thorough notes . But these old favorites seem to be my targets this time together with wines I always buy ( i.e. VCC )

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
If you’re interested in buying 2020s, and are not fans of wines with 14.5° or more, you need to be careful. There seems to be a lot more variation than usual this year, with wines from the same appellation going from 13 to 15°. In St. Julien for example, Branaire and Langoa are 14.5°, Ducru, Léo Barton, Poyferré, Lagrange and Beychecelle are at or around 13.5°, but Du Glana is 15°. Elsewhere, Haut Brion is also 15°.
Obviously these are samples and like all EP samples, they do not necessarily reflect the reality in the bottles on the shelves.
Overall on the Left Bank, there does seem to be a pattern of lower alcohol levels, which is good news, for me at least.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
I thought the 2019 Hosanna, along with the other Moueix properties, were priced high in 2019, so I’m not sure the pricing on the 2020 is a harbinger of things to come.

That’s true. Hosanna went only down by 10% last year. Most names were roughly 20-25% cheaper, some even more than 30% (Mouton -33%).

Taking Hosanna’s 2018 to 2020 price move as a guidance (roughly -15%) and applying that same logic to the other wines, we will see big price increases for many names. Mouton would increase its price by roughly 27%, Margaux and many other wines which were down around 20-25% would increase the price by roughly 10% vs 2019.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
I have to say I think Hosanna 2020 does look like it could be quite compelling, personally.

It’s decent vfm compared to back vintages, and scores quite highly (Suckling) too.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
You’re cherry-picking with Mouton. Overall, 2019 was 21% down, with a fair number of properties seeing more modest decreases in the <15% range. I also think basing off a 2018, a year some cheateaus hit 2010-like price highs, is not the best comparison. A better comparison for 2020 is vs. 2015-2016… and you can see 2020 Hosanna is about 30% cheaper than the '15-'16 market rate.

If you look at other Pomerols like Conseillante, L’Evangile, and Gazin, the 2019 release was within the market range of 2015-2016. 2019 Clinet and VCC are about 10-20% lower.

I’m not disputing the fact that Hosanna feels expensive at this price… but that’s pretty much the case for almost all of Pomerol. Anyway, we’ll find out soon enough.

posts.name: Jonathan Gr/-\|-|/-\/\/\
posts.cooked: In looking at GBP pricing on today’s release, I cant help but notice CB is only up 3% from last year’s release. But in USD its up roughly 20%. FX seems to be the main driver here, with the USD having depreciated in value vs both the EUR and GBP over the past year. I suspect this means we should expect to see USD price increases for 2020’s in the range of 15-20% which will be flat pricing from a European perspective (and if we don’t, that means the chateau would be actually cutting prices from last year). For the people that buy from Total Wine, you may want to also double-check the fine print, as I recall some folks saying they changed their terms and can charge you more if the currencies move unfavorably before you take delivery. This may mean you could see a price hike in your 2019 purchases retroactively depending on what that clause says.

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l
posts.cooked:
Officially on Total Wine

2020 Cheval Blanc

France > Bordeaux > Saint-Émilion/Satellites
98 Points: Spice, anis, rosemary, blackcurrant leaf, redcurrants, mint, peonies, the full array of aromatics are on display here, and there is an enveloping aspect to the fruit once you get to the body of the wine. The tannins are compact and powerful but they are wrapped in plump raspberry and blackberry brambled fruits. The wine feels full of life, with acidity that pulls the palate forward from the first moment, before austerity kicks in on the finish and closes things in, suggesting an extremely long life ahead. Hard to argue with this. Could go up after tasting in bottle, a potential 100 points.

  • Jane Anson, Decanter

The wine feels full of life, with acidity that pulls the palate forward from the first moment, before austerity kicks in on the finish and closes things in, suggesting an extremely long life ahead. Hard to argue with this. Could go up after tasting in bottle, a potential 100 points.

  • Jane Anson, Decanter

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
bordeaux-primeurs.net

Bordeaux Primeurs

Historique des prix de vente en primeur des vins de Bordeaux de 1980 à nos jours.

You then click on a link per “village” or zone and then get a list of wines (Margaux, Pauillac,…)
example: Château Haut-Brion - Pessac-Léognan
On this website, you have access to primeurs prices over the years (1980-2019). It clearly shows very high volatility year to year and surprising information with many ups and downs.
Some years are missing probably because the creator did not have all information.

Clearly, lower level wines have not inflated a lot: example Aiguilhe (castillon) 18.3€ in 2000, 18€ in 2018 and 14€ in 2019!
at a medium level Branaire Ducru: 2000 23.65 €, 2005 39€, 2010 55€, 2019 32.90€
Grand Puy Lacoste 2000 34.55€, 2005 53€, 2019 50.40 €

Most wine prices are lower in 2019 than 10 years earlier…

Just for clarification, the bet is whether specific wines in the 2020 vintage would appreciate over the next 10 years or not.

I don’t believe EP every year makes sense - 2017 is a clear example, 2018 might be tricky in the round - but I think specific wines in 2020 absolutely do. 2010 was, of course, peak bubble before it popped.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Just for clarification, the bet is whether specific wines in the 2020 vintage would appreciate over the next 10 years or not.
I don’t believe EP every year makes sense - 2017 is a clear example, 2018 might be tricky in the round - but I think specific wines in 2020 absolutely do. 2010 was, of course, peak bubble before it popped.

I have no doubt that they will increase in value over 10 years time. Quality seems to be there, prices seem to be resonable and most importantly inflationary forces that are dominating the fine wine market ofr 30 years are still there and will be in the next 10 years (growing middle class and wealth concentration at the top, ever more rich people). Picking Carmes HB (see secondary market development of the past few vintages) is an obvious winning choice. Margaux (on a roll, head to head and currently with a slight edge for the best 1er in the past few vintages) seems good too. Pichon Baron is knocking it out of the park and will likely continue to increase its quality (still I would have picked Lalande). I agree with the Canon over Clinet comment as Canon currently invests more in quality improvement. I’m impartial vs d’Issan and would have probably picked something different (Rauzan Segla? Trotanoy? Conseillante?) but I’m sure d’Issan will be higher as well (it got good ratings so far and Margaux/Pessac seem to be the strongest left bank appelations in 2020).

posts.name: M@tt R0bs0n
posts.cooked:
There is nothing here to create any kind of urgency to buy. On the contrary, the increasing price is directly responsible for me going out and buying more Palmer 2019. For some reason it is still close to the opening price.

Nothing so far suggests that 2020 is better than ‘19, or even it’s equal. For those people living in countries with weak currencies, and in view of the small increase in price, the net increase is about 20%. It is clearly a smarter move to look for unsold 2019 that are still around the opening price than buying 2020.

As a Canadian, I wish. I have about 110 bottles of 2019 on order… wish I could pickup a few more. You basically get 1 shot at buying EP and stuff sells out in minutes and you are usually capped at 6 bottles max per chateau unless there is anything left after the first ~90 minutes.

If I bring anything back from the US duty free, I try focus on back filling as the prices and selection are even worse.

If you are prepared to pay for storage, why not buy at opening prices in the US, and when the wines are ready bring them in drips and drabs?

In the long run, especially for long term storage, it would be more expensive. Looking anywhere from CAD $2.50-$3 per case per month … ~$30-36 per year from the places somewhat conveniently located.

2019 pontet was ~80USD for first release which, at the time, would have been around $108 CAD without adding any taxes or the 25% duty risk in 2019.

Ontario EP price was $159 CAD all in which is ~50% more, but after a ~1.5 years of storage, you would be worse off.

The best avenue would be purchase your top 60 bottles (or total 45 liters in various formats which is the max personal import limit) from a single regional area, go down and pickup in person and bring them across the border via a more liberal Province like Alberta, paying only the sales tax (13%).

Possibly still more work than its worth for EP.

I would say its much more worth while for back-filling!

Sticking with pontet-canet, the 2010 and 2009 came back to Ontario in April 2020 as a special release for $547!!! CAD for 750ml. You could pickup them up in the states for ~$235-250 USD… close to 2 for 1!

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
The psychology of release timing and pricing is potentially fascinating.

Release too early and you may have underpriced your product.
Release too late and the capital might be exhausted from other wines.
Release first and you might get caught out. Bit equally you might build a hype train and sell everything.
Release too late at an high price and you might come in under the general buzz of disillusionment

posts.name: Al3ks V3y
posts.cooked:
The psychology of release timing and pricing is potentially fascinating.

Release too early and you may have underpriced your product.
Release too late and the capital might be exhausted from other wines.
Release first and you might get caught out. Bit equally you might build a hype train and sell everything.
Release too late at an high price and you might come in under the general buzz of disillusionment

Fairly common issue for any type of limited / timed release / luxury consumer goods. Back in my consulting days I did some luxury retail (clothes, perfumes, watches, etc) and this was a big dilemma. It’s tricky since it requires a lot of competitor analysis, customer segmentation, marketing hype, and smart pricing to be successful. Even with modern analytics still a lot more guess work then pricing exercises for mass retail / consumer goods.

posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked:
Laroque $160 for 6 bottles.
So far I passed on everything but might buy this.
With David Suire as director their wines improved greatly.

Perhaps nice to share this as well, François Mitjaville (of Tertre Roteboeuf/ Roc de Cambes/ Domaine de Cambes) wrote me the following:

"Let us attempt an understanding of this vintage (it is still too soon to say much more).

Springtime was very wet, the summer hot and dry (but with some nice storms in mid-August), severe heatwaves in September (up until the 18th), causing slight dehydration in the berries and part of the foliage.
This was followed by rain showers and a return to cooler temperatures.

The harvest was the earliest that I have ever known….and I am no longer a young man, far from it! So, it was 21st September for Tertre Roteboeuf and some ten days later at Roc de Cambes.

The fruit, and hence the vintage, is dense and the acidity relatively low due to the heat of the summer; the rains have softened the tannins, which are expressive, aromatic, and on the palate the wine is fleshy.

There is also great freshness in the wine, the style of the fruit being bright and ripe, in some contrast to the preceding vintages where we were more in the realm of almost crystallised fruit, with a sensation of depth.
Warning! Tertre Roteboeuf, in an ‘indoor climate’, is deeper, while Roc and Domaine de Cambes, tempered by the Atlantic influence, are charmers.

This strikes me as a very fine vintage, full of long-term potential, yet promising to show very attractively in its youth."

posts.name: Hans-Peter Eisele
posts.cooked:
Freshness and low acidity is for me a slight disconnect.

I guess it’s the fruit that’s meant to be fresh and bright. If a wine has jammy (stewed) fruit, even with a ton of acidity, neither the wine nor the fruit will appear fresh, there’ll just be a more or less pronounced acidity (worst case you get some sweet-andsour play which is awful IMO). It annnoys me every time when wine critics say “fresh”, but mean “acidic”.

Cheers,
Hans-Peter

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
96.8 to 98.3 is such a small 1.5 pts difference on a 100 pts scale, true. But when you think of the absolute top of the fine wine world, which starts at 95 pts and goes to 100 points, it’s a huge difference.

But more important than focussing on the absolute pts difference is the ranking in my experience. We all know that every great vintage produces many great wines but just a few true legends which blow your socks off. It seems that Ausone/Mouton/Lafleur/Margaux/Trotanoy might be these wines but I know it very likely won‘t be Lafleur Petrus or Calon Segur.

It is best to experience that difference yourself. Do verticals and/or horizontals yourself, in a blind tasting setting and compare the results of your group to the consensus on CT. I do them regurarly and it‘s quite amazing how our group ranking at the top is so similar to the CT ranking (because of the legends that universally perform).

Max S. wrote:
Anyone a fan of Brane Cantenac? WineCellarInsider gives it their highest barrel tasting score for the label. I’ve only had a bottle of the 2000, but I absolutely loved it. Similarly, any thoughts on more moderately priced Bordeaux that might hit that same note?

We are huge fans of the Brane Cantenac. My wife thought the 2015 Brane was the best wine that she tasted at the UCG when they were pouring the 2015 vintage 3 years ago, and we have bought the '16, '18 and '19. We are also big fans of D’Issan, Domaine de Chevalier and Beau-Sejour Becot at a similar price point, stylistic differences aside.

posts.name: Paul @bbott
posts.cooked: Thanks for the St Estéphe notes Jeff, really looking forward to the arrival of the remaining articles.

Anyone a fan of Brane Cantenac? WineCellarInsider gives it their highest barrel tasting score for the label. I’ve only had a bottle of the 2000, but I absolutely loved it. Similarly, any thoughts on more moderately priced Bordeaux that might hit that same note?

Brane Cantenac is one of my favorite crus in Margaux (except for Margaux, Palmer and in recent vintages Durfort-Vivens+Rauzan-Ségla): it has the finesse and elegance you can expect from a Grand Cru of this AOC, and it has the structure to age to become a classic and delicious Bordeaux Grand Cru. The heart of Brane is located on the “plateau de Brane”, clearly one of the best terroir of the AOC, alongside the gravel slopes of Palmer and Margaux…

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Here’s an update on the top list (now incl. Martin). All wines with an average rating of 96.0 and higher.

The columns:

  1. alcohol level (not available for every wine)
  2. wine (if in stars not judged by critics who didn’t travel to Bdx)
  3. average score
  4. number of maximum points (i.e. highest bracket, which for Parker, as an example, would be 98 to 100)

Not every wine has been tasted and scored yet by every critic. 13 critics/publications (and one person from trade) are included. The critics (or publications) included are Quarin, Decanter, Bettane, Falstaff, Robinson, Beck, Revue Vins France, Lobenberg (all traveled to Bdx) and Parker, Suckling, Leve, Dunnuck, Martin (did not travel to Bdx).

Thanks everyone.

Staying on the Total Wine theme…they seem very light so far on the 2020 offering vs. past years. Any idea why?

If I had to guess… Foreign Exchange concerns. They may be hedging a bit on pricing. Typically they’re one of the lowest price EP retailers in the country. They’re almost dollar for dollar the same on pricing as K&L which is uncommon from my experience - some bottles are even more expensive than K&L (Langoa Barton is cheaper however at TW). So all that said, they might be trying to build some more margin in anticipation of a weaker dollar and/or inflation and are waiting for other major retailers to release pricing and stay at or below those marks. Just speculation of course!

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
I’m not so sure 2020 is a dud…it’s actually quite exciting to comb through as a collector who intends to drink the wine. That’s especially true if you’re not looking for trophies, but rather wines you could open mid-week, just because, or an excellent pairing for a Christmas meal. So far I’ve purchased Chateau Laroque St-Em ($32), took a chance on Tour St-Cristophe ($32), Lilian Ladouys ($20), Langoa Barton ($45) and Leoville Barton ($95). Only the Leoville Barton felt like a weak value proposition, but I’ve tried to buy this estate most years and it could be a really compelling wine to compare to the 2019 in time. I’ve also purchased more 2019 during this time too (Leoville Barton and d’Issan), and I intend to buy more 2020 as they’re released and I make sense of the vintage. (Possibly Lafon Rochet, Brane-Cantenac, Grand Puy Lacoste, d’Issan, Domaine Chevalier, more Langoa and possibly some more value right bank wines that have consensus on freshness).

I’m quite certain that most of these will be far more expensive in 5-10 years time when I’m beginning to even consider drinking the more approachable wines. At my age, I’ve hopefully got several more decades of buying EP and am happy to keep a pipeline of Bordeaux coming at the most affordable price possible. No one knows how each wine will progress in bottle over the years - and there’s beautiful wines in every vintage, even the weak ones.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
Panos just released his Top 50 list from 2020 EP for those interested : Top 50+ hits: Bordeaux 2020 from barrel | Wine Chronicles

I always find Panos’s reviews quite helpful, as he values freshness, finesse and good structure. He seems to be more optimistic than other reviewers on a number of second wines, which may spur me to check some out if the price is right.

Many thanks ! Overall, if you seek futures, 2019 is the one to buy, taking pricing into account, weakening dollar. But also quality wise for the Left Bank.

The “Top50+” evokes the best of the 2020s, so you may consider buying them, but in the Medoc you would generally find as much fun from 2019 for the very top, and more fun for mid-range and Cru Bourgeois. Cabernet Sauvignon - generally speaking - was smoother in 2019 in the Medoc. There are notable exceptions: Pontet Canet, Calon Segur are both terrific, for example.

The Right Bank is different in that the Merlots from colder soils were truly superb. Saint Emilion hasn’t been this consistent in a while. But even there, 2019 can be as good or close to the top 2020s, but not better.

As for second wines, those on the Right Bank generally better. Oftentimes the Left Bank second wines were far weaker, as if the less successful grapes were more obviously felt. The exceptions are in the Top50+.

All barrel samples, so final proof in bottle. Looking forward to tasting the 2019s later this year.

At my age, I’ve hopefully got several more decades of buying EP and am happy to keep a pipeline of Bordeaux coming at the most affordable price possible. No one knows how each wine will progress in bottle over the years - and there’s beautiful wines in every vintage, even the weak ones.

You can often buy dailies at better prices without EP, so it’s a dud in that sense.

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
I’m not so sure 2020 is a dud…it’s actually quite exciting to comb through as a collector who intends to drink the wine. That’s especially true if you’re not looking for trophies, but rather wines you could open mid-week, just because, or an excellent pairing for a Christmas meal. So far I’ve purchased Chateau Laroque St-Em ($32), took a chance on Tour St-Cristophe ($32), Lilian Ladouys ($20), Langoa Barton ($45) and Leoville Barton ($95). Only the Leoville Barton felt like a weak value proposition, but I’ve tried to buy this estate most years and it could be a really compelling wine to compare to the 2019 in time. I’ve also purchased more 2019 during this time too (Leoville Barton and d’Issan), and I intend to buy more 2020 as they’re released and I make sense of the vintage. (Possibly Lafon Rochet, Brane-Cantenac, Grand Puy Lacoste, d’Issan, Domaine Chevalier, more Langoa and possibly some more value right bank wines that have consensus on freshness).

I’m quite certain that most of these will be far more expensive in 5-10 years time when I’m beginning to even consider drinking the more approachable wines. At my age, I’ve hopefully got several more decades of buying EP and am happy to keep a pipeline of Bordeaux coming at the most affordable price possible. No one knows how each wine will progress in bottle over the years - and there’s beautiful wines in every vintage, even the weak ones.

You can often buy dailies at better prices without EP, so it’s a dud in that sense.

I’d love to see it…! 10 years of watching Bordeaux. I’ve yet to see pricing go down from first tranche EP at retail.

It also depends how you want your wine. As an quick example, I just paid $120 final for 01 Montrose in perfect condition and shared some bottles with forumites. Drinks great today, but could definitely still improve. I routinely see bottles from great vintage/producers at awesome prices. Would you rather have paid EP price for those and stored them yourself until today, or would you rather have backfilled? If it’s the former, any storage costs would offset much of your initial investment, no?

Having done this for many years, I think it’s good to do both. That’s sorta the beauty of Bordeaux, where you can generally get great deals EP in many vintages, while still picking up deals on recent releases, all the while scoring great backfills. Backfilling is even more wise today given the better conditions associated with wine delivery and storage these last 25 years, and the cost, like that 2001 Montrose, are often lower than new releases. I just bought yesterday a 2001 Trotanoy for $175, while new releases are double that. As much as I love Trotanoy, at 55 years of age, this decision is a no-brainer, to backfill over buying new. At y’all’s age, do both. And buy as many 375s as you can.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos

Max S. wrote:
Anyone a fan of Brane Cantenac? WineCellarInsider gives it their highest barrel tasting score for the label. I’ve only had a bottle of the 2000, but I absolutely loved it. Similarly, any thoughts on more moderately priced Bordeaux that might hit that same note?

We are huge fans of the Brane Cantenac. My wife thought the 2015 Brane was the best wine that she tasted at the UCG when they were pouring the 2015 vintage 3 years ago, and we have bought the '16, '18 and '19. We are also big fans of D’Issan, Domaine de Chevalier and Beau-Sejour Becot at a similar price point, stylistic differences aside.

Ed

Thanks Ed, I don’t often see it mentioned. Will be sure to check those 3 out!

Brane Cantenac was very good indeed, but I’d probably opt for the 2019. Margaux was less even in 2020. Palmer is terrific as expected but Chateau Margaux pulled a cat out of the hat and made a top wine in a vintage that will be celebrated more for Merlots and Saint Emilion. As for Brane, yes, it’s very good. But if the price is higher than 2019, I’d stick with 2019, or get back vintages. Rauzan-Segla is better and so is Issan, at least based on barrel samples I tried.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
Thanks Ed, I don’t often see it mentioned. Will be sure to check those 3 out!

Brane Cantenac was very good indeed, but I’d probably opt for the 2019. Margaux was less even in 2020. Palmer is terrific as expected but Chateau Margaux pulled a cat out of the hat and made a top wine in a vintage that will be celebrated more for Merlots and Saint Emilion. As for Brane, yes, it’s very good. But if the price is higher than 2019, I’d stick with 2019, or get back vintages. Rauzan-Segla is better and so is Issan, at least based on barrel samples I tried.

Good read Panos. Thank you! So, begging to be asked is, which wines outperform the 19’s in your opinion?

Thank you for reading! Thing is, generally speaking, 2020 has higher tannin than 2019. But not as much airy elegance, which is what I like. So 2020 may go a longer distance, but lacks the charm of the 2019s - again generally speaking - for the Left Bank. As for wines that “outperform” 2019, I can think of a few that come to mind, including a spectacular Pontet Canet and Calon Segur. Château Margaux hit a home run in 2020. Many stars from the Medoc are about as good, such as Pichon Longueville. Others display more tannic power certainly Lynch Bages and Pichon Baron. But what tips the scales for me for 2019 is the more refined tannins of the Cabernets. Take Issan, for example. As much as I love Issan in 2020, the blend was much more Cabernet Sauvignon dominated in 2019, because the Cabs were better. Lynch Bages also had a record amount of Merlot in 2020. The typical blend at Lynch Bages is at least 70% Cabernet Sauvignon. In 2020, it is but 60%. As for the Baron, the power of the tannin is amazing, but I prefer the 2019, as it conveys power, certainly, but with a touch more refinement and elegance. I will taste the 2019s from bottle later this year, and it will be great to see how they perform as final products. Certainly the 2020s will need time and after about 20 years, we may be saying “wow, this turned out better than I thought”. Time will tell. The Right Bank is different and there are so many terrific wines made from grapes of vines grown on limestone and clay soils that the density can be better in 2020 as compared to 2019, so it is more a question of price. In Wine Chronicles, you can see some of my preferences for Saint Emilion, which is a star appellation for 2020.

posts.cooked:
Brane Cantenac was very good indeed, but I’d probably opt for the 2019. Margaux was less even in 2020. Palmer is terrific as expected but Chateau Margaux pulled a cat out of the hat and made a top wine in a vintage that will be celebrated more for Merlots and Saint Emilion. As for Brane, yes, it’s very good. But if the price is higher than 2019, I’d stick with 2019, or get back vintages. Rauzan-Segla is better and so is Issan, at least based on barrel samples I tried.

Good read Panos. Thank you! So, begging to be asked is, which wines outperform the 19’s in your opinion?

Thank you for reading! Thing is, generally speaking, 2020 has higher tannin than 2019. But not as much airy elegance, which is what I like. So 2020 may go a longer distance, but lacks the charm of the 2019s - again generally speaking - for the Left Bank. As for wines that “outperform” 2019, I can think of a few that come to mind, including a spectacular Pontet Canet and Calon Segur. Château Margaux hit a home run in 2020. Many stars from the Medoc are about as good, such as Pichon Longueville. Others display more tannic power certainly Lynch Bages and Pichon Baron. But what tips the scales for me for 2019 is the more refined tannins of the Cabernets. Take Issan, for example. As much as I love Issan in 2020, the blend was much more Cabernet Sauvignon dominated in 2019, because the Cabs were better. Lynch Bages also had a record amount of Merlot in 2020. The typical blend at Lynch Bages is at least 70% Cabernet Sauvignon. In 2020, it is but 60%. As for the Baron, the power of the tannin is amazing, but I prefer the 2019, as it conveys power, certainly, but with a touch more refinement and elegance. I will taste the 2019s from bottle later this year, and it will be great to see how they perform as final products. Certainly the 2020s will need time and after about 20 years, we may be saying “wow, this turned out better than I thought”. Time will tell. The Right Bank is different and there are so many terrific wines made from grapes of vines grown on limestone and clay soils that the density can be better in 2020 as compared to 2019, so it is more a question of price. In Wine Chronicles, you can see some of my preferences for Saint Emilion, which is a star appellation for 2020.

Very insightful - enjoyed reading through your top 50 report as well, thanks!

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
I suspect score manipulation

Why, and what proof?

Proof? Proof is in the pudding: look. In fairness, it should be noted that several of presumably the best wines (Latour, Cheval Blanc, Petrus, Lafleur and Ausone) did not issue samples for tasting. Neal M. at least said that he thought the peaks of 2019 were higher than 2020; okay, fair enough. I’m still irritated that he changed his philosophy on giving out 100 pointers a few years ago, making it difficult to compare across years.

As for why, Galloni made a very big deal in his apologia for the preview option that high scores, in particular 100-pointers, were more damaging to consumer interest than the preview option; thus his rather silly comparison on the number of 100-pointers he gave out vs. other critics. So it would not surprise me if he made a particular point of not giving out any potential 100-pointers given the proximity.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
I suspect score manipulation

Why, and what proof?

The “why” would likely be preventing potential larger Chateau price increases and fast sell-outs due to “perfect” scores. The high scores drive the investment market. Keeping scores closer to the mean makes for a more drinker-friendly vintage.

The only wine that has really engaged me so far is Cheval Blanc, and that’s because 2019 moved a fair bit on the secondary market.

Manipulate scores down to hurt his chances to sell the preview option? I don‘t know. Maybe the more likely explanation is that 2020 was just not as good as the previous vintages. Quarin didn‘t award perfect scores either (and he did in 15/16/18/19), Neil too (and he did gave potentially perfect scores in the previous vintages too).

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
Concerns of excessive exuberance may also be a thought here too. After numerous good vintages, it becomes harder to continue scoring escalation. (unlike when you have an average vintage like 2017 when you score lower, giving you room to go higher on successive vintages). This is one of the issues with points in general, when you try and communicate complicated ideas like the quality and character of a vintage.

But… let’s all have some humility about what we can and do know. There will be wines across the board from each area, where sometimes 2018 is better, or 2019 better, or 2020 better. It seems like it’s a good to very good vintage. In time the difference may not be as qualitative, and we may appreciate the differences between the two. With the differences being neither good or bad, just different. I missed the EP boat in 2018, but will be buying broadly from 2019 and 2020.

posts.cooked:
Isn’t it “damned-if-you-do, damned-if-you-don’t” for AG?

If he had a whole bunch of 100 point scores, the critics would say he did that to “prove” the value of the preview for his new customers.
With no 100 point scores, the critics say he is doing that to “prove” that he is not influenced by the new model and/or to show he is a conservative critic.

Given the uproar/concern over his preview subscription, it seems to me no matter what his scores showed, they would likely be second-guessed by his critics.

posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked:
My offer for Lafleur €1,950 per case of 3 bottles; €1,310 per magnum

46% Bouchet (Cabernet Franc), 54% Merlot

A great Lafleur and one of the wines of the vintage. Dark and dense on the nose with a chocolate note from the Bouchet (Cabernet Franc) and hint of minerality. Whack of fruit and plentiful but really fine tannin on attack. Initially, broad, dense and full then tightens up and disappears into the distance. Drive and persistence and minerally freshness throughout. Enormous ageing potential." - 19/20 points Jancis Robinson

I was, to be honest, a little unsure of how the gravels here would react to the dry summer but the first hit of graphite and smoky berry fruit makes it clear Lafleur has taken the vintage in its stride. This is an excellent wine, barely showing its potential right now - it’s a big year, with knitted-down tannins, serious and powerful, not austere so much as layered, complex and full of hidden energy. The floral aromatics curl up after 10 minutes in the glass, as does bitter chocolate and crushed mint." - 98/100 points Decanter

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
Buy 2019. I think 2020 is deliberately priced to make people clear out the discounted 2019 stock thus reducing the risk of longer term price decrease.

2019 is certainly the best value play… but I’m not sure 2020 pricing is coupled to 2019 pricing. In 2019, between Covid, trade disputes, Brexit and everything else, I think the Bordelaise simply made a calculated decision to price these wines much lower, fearing that macro events might suppress demand. In 2020, many are cautiously optimistic - and many are expecting if not betting on relatively quick recovery. Future releases / tranches of 2019 will likely be much higher in price. K&L in the US seems to have already sold through most of their initial allocations. And Total Wine pulls futures (from what I’m aware) once the new EP hits. There are still other importers with remaining allocation through negociant, but I think we’ll see an immediate price increase for 2019 in the near future.

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
I dont agree. I know people who are buying a lot of 2019 back stock because of 2020 pricing. If 2019 is likely to be the cheapest in +/- 5 years, guess what everyone is gonna buy - 2019. Esp when its at least comparable to 2020. Farr have sold 36 bottles of 2019 Chevalier rouge in the last 12 hours alone. Lack of supply at one price point intrinsically means price increase!

K&L has already re-stocked on 2019 Leoville Barton Pre-Arrivals…and those sold out at higher prices as well…! ($90 last restock vs initial first tranche $75 I believe). I recently purchased 2019 Leoville Barton from Laguna Cellar at $75, so they can still be found from smaller importers/retailers who still have allocations from earlier tranches to sell - but those will sell through soon enough. It’s possible that if/when K&L obtains more allocation for 2019 Leoville Barton it could be the same price as the 2020 ($95).

Point being - we’re already seeing a huge advantage in 2019 for early tranches of EP. Not even a year later and we’ve seen that discount start to disappear relative to 2020 pricing. Some Forex could be in play - but the boat is likely going to have “sailed” here within the next few months I bet. Chateaux will undoubtedly increase prices 20-30%+ on later releases of 2019, especially if these are rated higher in bottle.

Despite all the discussion on this board through the last decade, my experience is that if you find a wine compelling, and enough of a value during early tranches of EP, buy it if you can afford it. You’ll either lack access to the same wine later on, and if it is accessible, it will likely come at a premium to EP. If you’re really lucky, you may be able to snap it up for 5-10% over initial EP, but most of the time it comes at 15%-30%-45% increase. It’s just like the stock market - something is too expensive so they don’t buy it, then it goes up in price over time, and they snap it up during a dip down the road thinking they got a good value…but the reality is they still bought well over the price of their first opportunity to buy.

posts.cooked:
I dont agree. I know people who are buying a lot of 2019 back stock because of 2020 pricing. If 2019 is likely to be the cheapest in +/- 5 years, guess what everyone is gonna buy - 2019. Esp when its at least comparable to 2020. Farr have sold 36 bottles of 2019 Chevalier rouge in the last 12 hours alone. Lack of supply at one price point intrinsically means price increase!

K&L has already re-stocked on 2019 Leoville Barton Pre-Arrivals…and those sold out at higher prices as well…! ($90 last restock vs initial first tranche $75-80 I believe). I recently purchased 2019 Leoville Barton from Laguna Cellar at $75, so they can still be found from smaller importers/retailers who still have allocations from earlier tranches to sell - but those will sell through soon enough. It’s possible that if/when K&L obtains more allocation for 2019 Leoville Barton it could be the same price as the 2020 ($95).

Point being - we’re already seeing a huge advantage in 2019 for early tranches of EP. Not even a year later and we’ve seen that discount start to disappear relative to 2020 pricing. Some Forex could be in play - but the boat is likely going to have “sailed” here within the next few months I bet. Chateaux will undoubtedly increase prices 20-30%+ on later releases of 2019, especially if these are rated higher in bottle.

Despite all the discussion on this board through the last decade, my experience is that if you find a wine compelling, and enough of a value during early tranches of EP, buy it if you can afford it. You’ll either lack access to the same wine later on, and if it is accessible, it will likely come at a premium to EP. If you’re really lucky, you may be able to snap it up for 5-10% over initial EP, but most of the time it comes at 15%-30%-45% increase. It’s just like the stock market - something is too expensive so they don’t buy it, then it goes up in price over time, and they snap it up during a dip down the road thinking they got a good value…but the reality is they still bought well over the price of their first opportunity to buy.

correct me if I misunderstand, but isnt that the point I’m making? EP 2019 first tranche prices are dissipating, you’re getting well into secondary market now, and I think within a year after 2019 EP hitting bottle, you’ll find (for the most part) pricing in line with 2020/2018


Personally, paraphrasing you slightly, I think if a wine is likely to sell out EP, I try to buy it (if its in my sphere of interest) - wines that sell out, increase in price.

I think I understand your point better now if I understand “the discounted 2019 stock” to mean early tranches of EP rather than the vintage at large. In which cases, I can see your point…! But I’m not sure 2020 has anything to do with it per se (i.e. sell 2020 at a premium to make 2019 look even better so you can rapidly increase pricing on 2019s). In their mind this may have been the plan all along. Sell 2019 at a discount below their real valuation when the macroeconomics are poor to stimulate demand, and when macroeconomics improve, correct the pricing to reflect the quality of the vintage. It’s possible that 2019/2020 essentially sell at identical pricing soon enough here. Possibly higher.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: Well, I think we can speculate about motivation, bu the impact/actions are clear right - 2019 was a good discount to perceived market value, 2020 is a small discount but not heavily so. A decent number of 2019 wines (non first growth) are still available at first tranche pricing, and I suspect 2020 EP will be really good at… clearing out stock of 2019 EP wines still available at first tranche

You’re leaving out a big aspect of the initial ‘price’ - those offers (for a US person) tended to come with the terms that any possible tariff changes would be pinned on the end buyer. As that tariff risk has receded, and as the global economy has recovered, yes the prices are higher, and initial purchasers look ‘clever’.

But sort of like a race car driver getting a faster car, by getting rid of roll cages and brakes and all that other stuff. There was more risk (than normal) involved for early purchasers, which seems to have gotten glossed over as time as gone by.

And with all the supply chain issues, retail bk’s one may not have really made it through the valley of risks yet to gloat over their mark to market ‘profits’ as measured by increasing offer prices.

posts.name: Whitmanlholt
posts.cooked: I would not buy any futures from Wally’s. I made that mistake a few times and had consistently bad experiences. The website is not user-friendly in terms of tracking open/unfilled orders. They do not do a good job coordinating delivery or even confirming when futures have arrived (for example, no email or other notification; as noted, the website has extremely poor functionality). Trying to coordinate pickup is a nightmare – unresponsive people confirming details, impossible to get people on the phone, absurd “who’s on first” miscommunication. It took me a month to actually get delivery of some 2016 Haut Brion after I repeatedly told it would be available “within 3 days for pickup.” I am never buying wine again from them generally, but the experiences I had with futures were particularly subpar. Stay away and order from K&L or Wine House.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Oh boy, if that was the worst of it… they lost thousands of dollars of orders (not just the wines, but the proof that we even ordered the wines). Had to find the old emails to demonstrate confirmation of order, then they tried to fill the wines based on their existing stock. It probably took 2-3 months total to get it sorted between convincing them we bought the wines, getting the stock filled, and getting refunds processed (Refunds processed at original purchase price, not replacement cost I’d even note). I cant remember how much the purchase price of the lost wines were, but somewhere between 5 and 10K USD.

If you have any futures from wallys outstanding prior to 2018 Bordeaux, you need to get a lawyer.


Apparently they changed ordering/inventory systems at some point in time and didnt bother to check data integrity. If you still have outstanding EP, you better hope you still have your original invoices.

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
It’s not about what a critic likes or not as much as their ability to describe the experience so the reader knows what they’re getting with the wine.

This is a more fair version of the point I was trying to make, but it would be lovely to find I generally agree with someone and rely on scores for quick generalizations (selfishly).

I’ve found that regional wine with expected profiles has the same issue as certain categories of spirits. If you are a bourbon reviewer, how often can you list oak/vanilla as a major tasting note? It makes the reviews look amateurish if you drop it in every review. Furthermore, there is a wide spectrum of belief in what constitutes “too much oak” to the point it is worth mentioning. Wine is a different beast in terms of complexity but suffers from the same problem.

In an ideal world, I’d still get the beautiful descriptions from folks like you, but there would be supplemental information about the wine based on some international standard (similar to sake information, but for critics to include with reviews based on perception). It would have to just be a few quick hitter ratings for things like wood, concentration, acidity, etc., but it would mean that, instead of comparing “a scintillating bounty of rich stewed kumquats” to “a lavish presentation of sun-kissed kiwis”… we could see that one was a 5/5 for concentration and the other was a 3/5. Then the reviews make a lot more sense.

It’s not about what a critic likes or not as much as their ability to describe the experience so the reader knows what they’re getting with the wine.

I’m about as far removed from a traditionalist as you can get. I think Mr. Alfret and a few others will back me up on that😁. But I’d like to think when reading my notes, I’m able to convey what a wine is like, or not.

Tour Saint Christophe is one of the strong value wines, mixing high quality with fair prices. But you can find others in St. Emilion. I am not aware of how much most wines cost, however. Look for Laroque and Sansonnet and others. I am working on my appellation reports now, publishing one a day. I am almost finished with the Left Bank.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
It’s not about what a critic likes or not as much as their ability to describe the experience so the reader knows what they’re getting with the wine.

In an ideal world, I’d still get the beautiful descriptions from folks like you, but there would be supplemental information about the wine based on some international standard (similar to sake information, but for critics to include with reviews based on perception).

I cannot speak for others. But do you read my site? You can find supplemental information for almost 1,000 wineries on my site.

It would have to just be a few quick hitter ratings for things like wood, concentration, acidity, etc., but it would mean that, instead of comparing “a scintillating bounty of rich stewed kumquats” to “a lavish presentation of sun-kissed kiwis”… we could see that one was a 5/5 for concentration and the other was a 3/5. Then the reviews make a lot more sense.

Again, I am a little bit lost on your request. But I will hazard a guess. As for the amount of oak, blends, alcohol levels, they can vary from year to year. Personally, I do not have the time to enter all that info, except for the top maybe 50-100 wines. With those wines, you get picking dates, yields, blends, ABV & pH. This year, I tasted 900 wines, though, in most vintages, it is closer to 600. While I empathize with your wishes, how is that possible? That is a lot of time, which I do not have, and a Herculean amount of effort.

IMO, the value of a good reviewer is 3 things. The ability to convey what the wine is like. Consistency, meaning, they like what they like because they like it, or not, for different styles of wine. They are capable of finding joy and excitement in wines and share their enthusiasm so that readers also want to taste, and even buy those wines.

That’s just me. YMMV…

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: The difference between a superb 60 dollar wine and a 600 dollar wine is small. It is so small that few people will detect the difference in a blind tasting. Therefore it is silly from a rational standpoint to pay 600 dollars for a bottle of wine when you can get the same or almost the same for 10% of the price. But many wine lovers do not act rational but emotional. Because a 600 dollar wine does impress more on the table than a 60 dollar bottle. And that big impression is the enemy of any objectivity. Not to talk about the fact that the 600 dollar bottle does promise more upside potential on the secondary market. Maybe it is worth 1.200 in 10 years. Because it is so much more desirable than the 60 dollar bottle. Not due to its inner quality but its rarity.

posts.cooked:
The difference between a superb 60 dollar wine and a 600 dollar wine is small. It is so small that few people will detect the difference in a blind tasting. Therefore it is silly from a rational standpoint to pay 600 dollars for a bottle of wine when you can get the same or almost the same for 10% of the price. But many wine lovers do not act rational but emotional. Because a 600 dollar wine does impress more on the table than a 60 dollar bottle. And that big impression is the enemy of any objectivity. Not to talk about the fact that the 600 dollar bottle does promise more upside potential on the secondary market. Maybe it is worth 1.200 in 10 years. Because it is so much more desirable than the 60 dollar bottle. Not due to its inner quality but its rarity.

I can say with confidence of many, many, many, many blind tasting that your statement and generalization is wrong. I usually found a strong correlation between price and quality.

Best examples are our larger horizontal 10y on tastings on Bordeaux (everybody scores the wines blind and before the wines are being discussed):

Best wines (group average scores) in 2010? 1) Mouton, 2) Margaux, 3) Le Pin, 4) VCC, 5) Cheval Blanc. Weakest wines (not corked/oxidized)? 58) Moulin Haut Laroque, 57) Domaine de Chevalier, 56) Pape Clement, 55) La Sergue, 54) Pavie Maquin.

Best wines in 2009? 1) Palmer, 2) Cheval, 3) Margaux, 4) Mouton, 5) VCC, 6) Lafleur. Weakest ones? 48) Faugeres, 47) Dubois Grimon, 46) Clos Fourtet, 45) G Acte, 44) Gazin, 43) Calon Segur.

Of course you have to compare apples to apples (and not oranges). A Domaine de Chevalier in a great vintage like 2016 will be the better wine than a Mouton in a weak vintage (and mediocre phase for the Chateau) like 2004. Cellartracker scores show that beautifully Sometimes, cheaper wines are great. A lot of times, expensive wines are not that great but to generalize and say that there is no difference people would detect, is just wrong.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: And I can say that I tasted with some of the best and experienced tasters (some were pretty well known critics) blind for some years and the most expensive wines were not always in the group of the best. Sometimes yes, sometimes no. Some wines yes, some wines no. The group I tasted with was called Grand Jury Europeen and members were people like Michel Bettane, Bernard Burtschy (wine writer for Le Figaro), Peter Moser (Falstaff), Masters of wine and several Best Sommeliers of the world. So I think there was a good panel of quality and expertise.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler

Bring any of these guys the Margaux or Mouton 2009 against any 60 dollars 2009 Bordeaux and they will all see the difference. 100% of them. It’s about comparing apples to apples and about your generalization that almost nobody can detect the difference.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
It is not hard to find wines the equal of or better than others costing three times, four times, ten times as much. And on forums such as this, we seek out value.

And the vagaries of the market place make this easy, where pricing is based not just on perceived quality but other things like rarity, style, fashion and reputation. But there are wines which cost $600 which are better than their $3000 counterparts, and in some ways, they too will be a value.

I would expect that any sensible person will try and max out their purchases based on what is important to them. If they buy a Le Pin for $3000, they may be serve it to a group that has never tasted Le Pin before, and for them, there is the value. Rarity and friendship.
Not bad reasons.

posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked:
Both Jurgen and Andy is correct. It comes down to “all depends”. What vintage, at what age of the wine you are drinking it, etc. Also, many drinkers are “programmed” to what is “good”. They were taught and/or influenced to like certain characters of wines and will choose those wines as “better”. I’m simplifying my explanation but hope my point is understandable.

Just want to add that using CT scores as proof of quality is not always the best gauge.

It is not hard to find wines the equal of or better than others costing three times, four times, ten times as much. And on forums such as this, we seek out value.

And the vagaries of the market place make this easy, where pricing is based not just on perceived quality but other things like rarity, style, fashion and reputation. But there are wines which cost $600 which are better than their $3000 counterparts, and in some ways, they too will be a value.

I would expect that any sensible person will try and max out their purchases based on what is important to them. If they buy a Le Pin for $3000, they may be serve it to a group that has never tasted Le Pin before, and for them, there is the value. Rarity and friendship.
Not bad reasons.

Honestly I don’t see much distance between the points of view being argued. Latour has unlimited resources, the very best personnel, plots of land that have a track record centuries long . . . if it was not
more reliable in producing good to great wine something would be desperately wrong.

But in nearly every vintage, there are wines produced that are its equal, or nearly so, at 1/10th or at least 1/5th the price. Wines that, served blind, would present differently, but not as qualitative strangers. EVERY vintage, even the blockbusters.

Some will pay the freight for the big boys because they can do so effortlessly and want the easiest path to excellent wine, some will pay the freight because they want to impress. There’s nothing wrong with that; it is, after all, their money.

Some of us might be able to afford to do that but find sport in ferreting out the wines in each vintage that fight above their weight class and some just have a principled objection to spending the money required to fill our cellars with 1st growths or grand crus. There are no wrong answers to this test.

posts.name: C Chen
posts.cooked:
Both Jurgen and Andy is correct. It comes down to “all depends”. What vintage, at what age of the wine you are drinking it, etc. Also, many drinkers are “programmed” to what is “good”. They were taught and/or influenced to like certain characters of wines and will choose those wines as “better”. I’m simplifying my explanation but hope my point is understandable.

Just want to add that using CT scores as proof of quality is not always the best gauge.

CT cannnn be extremely helpful, but similar to critic discussion above, it is much more effective if you find a large group of individuals who seem to share your tastes and “fab” they so you get look for relevant reviews (as opposed to pure scores)…but absent that being possible, a wine with 50 reviews and a consistently high average score is at the very least a crowd pleaser

Sure, but a crowd-pleaser doesn’t necessarily mean something I’d want to drink, especially when it comes to Bordeaux!

Take for example 2009 Clos Fourtet, which has a 94.4 average on CT. While I tend to like a much broader range than John Gilman, his score of 77-79 is a pretty good indication that this is going to taste like an aussie shiraz monsta. To your point about relevant reviews, there are some tasters that I trust to inform much better than the overall scores – eg. sirpat00 and cailles – who posted notes from a 10 years on blind tasting of 2009s, which had Clos Fourtet as 46th out of 48 wines.

CT is basically just Yelp. I follow a few people (Northup being one of them, xo) and mostly ignore general reviews. If reviews mattered, I’d have a cellar full of Sauternes.

Rauzan Segla at €66 - up 22.2%!!

Oof. That’s a real kick in the nuts. RS was on my very short list of things to buy this year. Why bother when there’s a glut of the 2019 still out there for $75… disappointing.

If you like Rauzan Segla, why is $80 unreasonable? IMO, Rauzan Segla is perhaps the third best wine in Margaux. It offers fabulous style, character and it ages for decades. Where can you find wines of that quality in other world-class appellations for $80?

If $80 is too much money for a bottle, I get it. But in comparison with other top wine wines from all over the world, where are you getting better value?

FWIW, my guess is, 2020 Rauzan should compete with 2015 in quality, making it a better wine than 2019. But that is only a guess.

FWIW, my guess is, 2020 Rauzan should compete with 2015 in quality, making it a better wine than 2019. But that is only a guess.

What do you consider 2nd best Margaux wine?

Palmer, followed by Rauzan Segla and today, Brane Cantenac is next.

As an appellation, Margaux, prior to 2015, some, if not several estates were not making the best wine possible. But today that is no longer the case. Because so many vineyards were not making top-level wines, I think the appellation has been inexpensive, vis a vis other major Left-Bank appellations. In the years to come, as a guess, I suspect you will see more new vintage price appreciation in Margaux.

This is from the DrinksBussines

Gruaud Larose (St Julien; 79% Cabernet Sauvignon; 17% Merlot; 4% Cabernet Franc; a final yield of 31 hl/ha; aging for 18 months in oak barrels, 95% of which are new; 13.2% alcohol). Tasted at the chateau with Nicolas Sinoquet. This is shockingly purple, with a wonderfully punk pink luminous rim. A lovely rather classical St Julien nose of graphite and cedar-encrusted black and blue berry fruit, with hazelnut shell and vibrant floral elements – above all, violets and irises.

On the palate, like the 2019, this is impressively dense and compact if a perhaps little more lithe and supple. There is great depth and yet an aerial lightness, clarity and levity to this which I really like. The finish is exquisite – very pure, focused and elegant. If there is a slight evolution here it is towards a somewhat more austere version of Ducru, with great depth and mid-palate concentration yet plenty of brightness and lift. An excellent wine and a great follow-up to the brilliant (if not widely tasted) 2019.

posts.name: Hamza Mechbal
posts.cooked:
French critiques tasted the 2020 Gruaud Larose

Bettane & Desseauve : 96-97/100
Revue du Vin de France : 93-95/100 « L’un des plus denses de l’appellation, il libère beaucoup de volume en bouche et un fruité noir. La finale est serrée, étirée et le bois le marque encore. Belle évolution à prévoir. »
Dupont (Le Point) : 16,5/20 « Fruits rouges, bouche souple, juteuse, tendue, serrée, tanins présents, belle matière »
Quarin : 95/100 « Nez très aromatique, fin, fruité, pur et complexe. Délicat en entrée de bouche, minutieux dans sa texture, raffiné au toucher, accompagné d’un fruité éclatant, le vin grandit en fin de bouche, long, sèveux et noble. »
Burtschy : 96-97/100 « Superbe Gruaud-Larose avec une vinification magistrale qui a dompté les excès du millésime avec une matière première certes de qualité, mais dont il fallait capter l’essentiel. […] Le vin est dense et serré avec de la fraîcheur, de la longueur, joli merlot, finale de chocolat noir. Au top niveau. »

and the 2019

Bettane & Desseauve : 97/100 « Corps parfaitement équilibré, tannin velouté ou soyeux, bouquet raffiné et aristocratique, encore sur sa réserve de jeunesse. Promis à un très grand avenir. C’est toujours le meilleur rapport qualité prix des seconds crus classés de Saint-Julien. »
Revue du Vin de France : 96-98/100 « Le fruit est magnifiquement enveloppé, avec des tanins suaves, gainés et surtout une finale qui explose et libère des notes de fruits noirs mûrs. Un vin charnu et très bien constitué. »
Quarin : 95/100 « Entrée en bouche caressante, un tantinet discrète, puis le vin se réveille à partir du milieu de bouche. Il se développe alors très aromatique, savoureux, noble dans son toucher et ses arômes, avant de s’étirer subtil et profond sur des tannins fins dans une grande finale. »
Dupont (Le Point) : 16.5/20 « Épices, fruits noirs, bouche large, gourmande, relevée en finale. »

posts.name: M@tt R0bs0n
posts.cooked:
If you like Rauzan Segla, why is $80 unreasonable? IMO, Rauzan Segla is perhaps the third best wine in Margaux. It offers fabulous style, character and it ages for decades. Where can you find wines of that quality in other world-class appellations for $80?

If $80 is too much money for a bottle, I get it. But in comparison with other top wine wines from all over the world, where are you getting better value?

FWIW, my guess is, 2020 Rauzan should compete with 2015 in quality, making it a better wine than 2019. But that is only a guess.

2020 Rauzan is out at $100, not $80. If it were $80 I would easily pick up half a case like I did in 2015, 2016 and 2019. I don’t think $100 is unreasonable, but what people are reacting to is that you can get the 2019 (which presumably is as good or nearly so) for 25% less. Put another way, would I rather put money in half of case of 2020, or 8 more bottles of 2019?

My bad. I thought I read in this thread 66 Euros. Wines and vintages are always a choice. Except for mailing list customers or highly allocated wines, I do not know anyone buying successive vintages.

As most people are not drinking these wines for at least 10-20 years after release, by the time you open them, what you paid today, which will turn into “Back in the day” pricing, what you paid in 2021, 22, 23, it won’t matter, especially at $100 or less wines that age.

The ex-chateau did come out at 66 EUR which appears to be translating close to $100 USD ex: Buy Chateau Rauzan-Segla 2020 wine online | Millesima

posts.name: William Gladstone
posts.cooked:
The Bordeaux negociant owned by CHANEL is our main contact, promoter and agent of sorts,
they arranged a tasting of very good assortment of the region of about 50/btls of the 2020 vintage.
We had a good assortment of the region and without 1st Growth, a well chosen assortment
of stature.
We held the tasting at a local Honolulu restaurant in the afternoon with a number of wine professionals and a few
private collectors - this was several weeks back.
We also selected some important Chateau bottles to send to some people who were unable to attend the tasting -
Maui fine wine retailer, local private collector who regularly drinks fine Bordeaux - Myself and I think
everyone else was quite surprised at just how the wines shined, so many of the wines seemed so luxurious.
I’ve always felt when I attend these En Primeur tastings - “I cannot tell what this will evolve into after the stages
that take place.” This was the very first time I not only felt I could see through the fog, I could taste something
particular about the vintage and on some instances - the particular Chateau.
Everyone jumped out at something truly extraordinary about Chateau Kirwan, there was something so special
each of the very experienced buyers noticed this and I’ve followed through with purchasing quite a bit
and contacting the Chateau that I’d like to work with them on a tour in Hawaii and perhaps some U.S. cities,
it was that special.

There is a local gentlemen - a private collector who for several decades he regularly drinks the finest Bordeaux.
We sent him a bottle of the 2020 Pichon Baron. His comments say it better than I have:

Good morning, William. WOW ! I must say, drinking the 2020 bottle of Pichon Baron last night was a more enjoyable experience than I thought possible. Even though the wine is so young, after my first sip my first thoughts were, “yum, beautiful freshness, pure and crisp, firm velvety texture, vivacious, a quintessdential Pauillac whose deep purple color should develop and age beautifully .”

The only other time I have had this direct a emotional ‘hit’ from a young grape is several years back, a 2ndry growth of grapes
in the fields of DRC. I picked bunches of grapes that were not picked , I walked the fields of RSV, LA TACHE and finally RC.
I spent the following week plus enjoying the amazing quality of the grapes particularly of RC.
Again, as I stated at the start of this post, I’ve always wondered are these people who write the reviews really honest and speaking
from knowledge when they make some statements - “good for 30 years” this wine will need 8 years to open" - and so many other statements
of ‘fact’.
I can say I’d be so surprised if that very special electric or ethereal quality I found in some of the juice-
does not turn out to produce one of the truly outstanding vintages for many of the wines.
Which is why we are only buying some of our allocations and not selling any as of yet.

Its interesting that the comments I read in this post are mostly like grocers who can weigh everything- as if it is entirely quantitative. The price of one vintage and the other.
I still remember some specific sensations of some wines, the thrill I experienced of a 1982 Pichon, the amazement of some 2005 Ramonet.
That is the quest that has kept me on this path…
immeasurable & , sensual memories that live on as tangible as any real world experience I’ve enjoyed.

I’m finding more and more of certain purchases, as I realize what view aging provides, I am not offering for sale certain wines.
