posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
19’ Lafite was selling for $599.97 on wine.com earlier, now sold out. 2020 listed at $699.97. At least the 2020 EP campaign is helping to sell the 19’ stock still left for sale.

And one would assume they will try to clear out the '20s next year by listing '21 at $799.97. People with enough money to buy anything really do love a bargain. /s (just a joke!)

I feel like some are rewarding too much cleverness to the pricing of this year’s releases. Lafite Rothschild '16 and '18, based on wine-searcher, were both at around $699 a year after EP, and it seems they feel they can price it that way from the start. Winemakers and customers last summer were all in the middle of freaking out about tariff/political uncertainty, and that year wasn’t so much a reset as an anomaly due to a confluence of multiple global crises.

I’m not sure if there is an overall trend, but I found it interesting that a number of 2019 futures showed a very sharp decrease in availability between roughly December of 2020 and February of 2021 (via wine-searcher charts). I’m guessing this was largely based on likelihood that tariffs would be removed or reduced, but this spike in demand may have been why we are seeing the significant increase in prices for '20. I don’t think it has anything to do with clearing out the '19s as I’m sure they are already confident those will take care of themselves.

posts.name: Brian Befano

Random comment but given these wines will not be drinkable until another 15-25 years…does price really matter. Assuming one is buying for self consumption!

The people with skin in the game (Chateau owners) certainly think pricing matters.

I don’t really understand your comment…but if you are saying “sellers want a high price”…of course. I was speaking about buyers.

It’s a two sided market, every seller needs a buyer, how would it matter to one party and not the other?

Demand for wine is highly elastic because the product is a luxury and there are many substitute products on the market, i.e. other Chateaus. I think this point is made clear in the amount of effort and expense that people who are most impacted by price, the owners, put into setting that price. If adding 10% to their product didn’t cause a serious change in consumer behavior than it would be less valuable to sweat those details in EP.

To the larger point, I am building a cellar for future consumption. I am trying to drink X bottles per year in 20 years, so I need to buy Y bottles every year and I have a budget of Z dollars, so my average cost per bottle needs to be Z/Y. So if I spend more than Z/Y on one bottle it means I have to spend less per bottle on something else to maintain the same average cost per bottle across all my purchases. I’m trying to solve that for highest quality and enough variety to be interesting.

So if I spend more than Z/Y on one bottle it means I have to spend less per bottle on something else to maintain the same average cost per bottle across all my purchases. I’m trying to solve that for highest quality and enough variety to be interesting.

In 20 years it will be irrelevant, the only thing that will matter will be quality of what you bought. My view! On a slightly tangent note…comparison to 2019 bordeaux (covid year) is also irrelevant. But these are my views!

How do you not pay attention to price when you try to purchase quality? If price didn’t matter we would all have cellars full of first growths. I’m confused… The rest are non sequiturs.

I understand you are trying to make a point, but I am not suggesting that price of first growths vs. grocery store wine doesn’t matter (if you can’t afford it now you can’t afford it later). I am just suggesting that in 20 years a 15-20% increase on quality wines will not matter. Particularly if that increase is compared to a once in a life time covid year. If you feel different so be it… see you in 20 years!

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
As someone who just got into EP last year, this is shaping up to be a very disappointing campaign.

People have been grumbling about EP since around 2005 that I can recall,

Oh no, this has been an annual sport since the beginning of time. I know a few old-timers that passed on 1982, thinking 82 Mouton was insane at $35. They purchased 1961 for about $10 or less. 82 was a massive increase over 1978.

For the First Growths, roughly, 1989/1990 was 50% more than 82. 95/96 was 50% over 89/90. 2000 was 50% over 95/96 and 05 was 40% over 00 etc.

It is all part of the game.

As long as there is an increase in wealth and demand, prices will rise to seek that demand. Buyers will adjust and pass or pay. The days when anyone could make money buying EP overnight are long over. Wines are priced for a hoped-for fair-value to end-consumers that will be happy paying that price to drink it. Some wines will increase in value over the next 18 months, most will remain flat, others will increase.

That is all part of the game.

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
I know a few old-timers that passed on 1982, thinking 82 Mouton was insane at $35. They purchased 1961 for about $10 or less. 82 was a massive increase over 1978.

Just for the sake of interest and forgiving the back-of-the-napkin Google math, $10 in 1961 translates to around $90 in today’s dollars. If 2020 MR is going to be $700, that means prices are roughly 7.8 times what they used to be, adjusted for inflation. I completely agree with previous comments about more wealth in the world (see more people + Bordeaux is a fixed plot of land), but it is interesting to note that median income in the US has only gone up 1.5 times, adjusted for inflation, and the world population has only gone up 2.5 times since '61.

People starting off today are at a distinct disadvantage compared to previous generations (from a US perspective at least) when trying to learn about or build a cellar with these fine wines. Business is business, so no judgement here, but this might be why younger US demographics (from my view) are largely anti-Bordeaux, anti-decades-long-aging-required-purchases, and are looking to things like natural wine and lesser heralded regions, which is in some ways changing the palate of future generations of wine drinkers.

This is true. But you also need to keep in mind that in those days, maybe, 2-3 vintages a decade were good to great. Meaning, less good wines to choose from. Also, in those days, after the very best estates, there was a massive drop in quality. Even many of the Second Growths, most of Pomerol, Saint Emilion and Pessac Leognan were making so-so wines in the majority of vintages.

Today, that is not the case.

Not only are vintages consistently better, but the majority of estates today are producing better wine than ever. You also need to consider that today, there are numerous 2nd, 3rd, 5th growths, etc., that are making better wine on a consistent basis than most of the First Growths were making, except in the best vintages during the 50s, '60s, '70s, '80s and '90s.

So, while it is much more money to buy the very top echelon of wines, you do not need to spend that much money to drink extraordinary world-class wine.

With that in mind, are today’s consumers worse off? Not in my opinion.

What do you guys think?

posts.cooked:

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Amazing that today, most estates today are making great wine and nearly all vintages are considered “great.” Yet price increases continue to substantially outpace inflation. Unlike, say from 1961->1982, in which $35 Mouton was roughly equivalent to 1961, correcting for inflation.

I think Bordeaux buyers are almost certainly worse off today than they were 20 years ago.

If you only want to buy Mouton, yes, you are in worse shape. Are you only a First Growth buyer? If it is Pauillac, both Pichon’s are making wine at First Growth levels today for 1/3 to 1/4 the price. If that is too much, Batailley has never made better wine. They are a classified growth selling for maybe 1/12 of Mouton. I can list other examples in every appellation. Unless consumers have to buy Mouton, I think they are better off today. But that is just me. YMMV…

Not that it matters to the consumer, and it shouldn’t because it is the price consumers are asked to pay, or not, that counts. But wine prices are not related to inflation. They are based on supply and demand.

As a guess, during the '60s, maybe Bordeaux was exported to 30 countries. Today 90 or more countries buy Bordeaux. That does not even take into consideration the growth in population or the wealth factor. More people with more money is not a recipe for lower prices. Add to that the fact that in 1982, Mouton could have produced 30,000 cases of wine, and today, it is possible only half that amount is produced in an average vintage.

When you add up that today, most of the best estates make half the wine they used to, selling it to double the population in 2 to 3 times as many countries and you are where you are.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
But to your earllier point, historically maybe 2-3 good vintages a decade - this decade, no one would argue 15/16/18/19, arguably 20, perhaps 12/14 as well. So per vintage volume may be halved, but decade productions of ‘great wine’ is substantially higher - probably more than the 2x factor needed to compensate for the halving of per-vintage production?


The thing is, its not a simple demand/supply system. The way its set up entirely removes that. The secondary market mostly is, dont get me wrong, but the chateau with the negociants, state funding, allocations, and merchants arent a standard free and fair market.

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked:
Sorry, but the talk of “Best ever?” doesn’t really make me think that I’m any better off now than I would’ve been 20 years ago. If you’re asking me to compare the two eras, then yeah, I think I would be a first growth only buyer given the two options.

I think we’ve had this discussion before, but year to year bordeaux prices are not based on supply and demand. The Bordelais get it wrong fairly often. They got it wrong in 2011, 2012, 2013, 2017, and in my opinion, 2018 as well. Prices were also wrong in 2009 and 2010, but it took Chinese consumers a few years after the fact to realize it. Of course, some chateau or negociants will dump extra stock via EU supermarkets, auction, airlines, etc. Or, wines will continue to languish in negociant warehouses. This is not a true free market, it’s a cartel. Let’s call it what it is.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
This is not a true free market, it’s a cartel. Let’s call it what it is.

Of course it’s a free market. That is why prices fall as well as rise or remain stagnant There is no obligation or need for anyone to buy Bordeaux, let alone any specific Château.

Bordeaux is the most transparent wine region into world. You know how much wine is produced each year. You know the wholesale price, prices of previous vintages etc are readily available. You know exactly how much money your retailer is making on your purchase.

Can you name any other wine region that provides the number of cases produced along with the wholesale price ?

How is this not an open and free market?

Of course you can say you don’t know how many cases were sold, or where they were shipped to. But can you name any winery, let alone wine region that provides that information openly?

Bottom line, at least for me, I’m only willing to pay what it’s worth to me to drink, regardless of how many they held back or sold.

There are a lot of differences, and I hyper-distilled the economics without much concern for anything cultural or technological. There are many things that are much better now. Technology makes things accessible across the world, and people understand more about how to store wine, etc. We have access to wine from many places, not just France and basket-woven bottles of chianti. That said, most even pretty enthusiastic wine enthusiasts will never drink a first growth Bordeaux. The fact that there are more and better-known winemakers from all over making top class wine also should have some impact on the rise in prices. Bordeaux in '61 wasn’t competing against Napa, or Italy (in most American markets), or high-end small batch spirits, or craft beer, etc. Bordeaux was Bordeaux (I am just working on stories as I wasn’t around, but my impression is there was really only one premium wine region in the world, and it was Bordeaux).

From an American perspective, we do understand wine better than we ever have, and it is easier to casually work our way into good wine if we have the money and time. We’ve gone in many both misguided and beneficial directions, from accepting blueberry wine and old-school white zin at parties to being shunned for that, to hating all merlot because of a movie, back to pink wine can be okay again, etc. The emergence of scores has definitely shaped how Americans value wine, but many are stuck thinking a value bottle of wine needs to be under $15 in perpetuity and don’t recalibrate that expectation even after 20+ years of shopping that way.

Today, we are spoiled for choice and have great value options from many places in the world. So, in that regard, we are better off. But if your measure is over learning about and developing a cellar of top class Bordeaux, I don’t think it can be better than it was then. There is a significant barrier, both financially and otherwise, to tasting back-vintages of top class Bordeaux so that you can learn, form opinions, and sink money into an investment that can’t be enjoyed for 20 years. Sure, they can be helicoptered to your doorstep with an app, but it is just more expensive with other great great values competing against it. I look at my friends, and zero of them are buying anything to put in a “cellar” for more than a year or two, while my parents at my age were cracking first/second growth Bordeaux and top tier Burgundy on the reg. Everyone I know who has tried top tier wine is either in the business or considers Caymus their top tier wine. I had a father who collected the stuff, so I’ve been incredibly lucky.

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked:
This is not a true free market, it’s a cartel. Let’s call it what it is.

Of course it’s a free market. That is why prices fall as well as rise or remain stagnant There is no obligation or need for anyone to buy Bordeaux, let alone any specific Château.

Bordeaux is the most transparent wine region into world. You know how much wine is produced each year. You know the wholesale price, prices of previous vintages etc are readily available. You know exactly how much money your retailer is making on your purchase.

Can you name any other wine region that provides the number of cases produced along with the wholesale price ?

How is this not an open and free market?

Of course you can say you don’t know how many cases were sold, or where they were shipped to. But can you name any winery, let alone wine region that provides that information openly?

Bottom line, at least for me, I’m only willing to pay what it’s worth to me to drink, regardless of how many they held back or sold.

Officially, it’s a free market in that there’s minimal government control. But I should say rather than the pricing is far more removed from supply/demand forces than any other wine region due to the negociant system in which the chateaus have automatic buyers at whatever price they set. Changes in end consumer demand take years to work their way back up to the chateau who set the prices. Which is why it’s so frustrating for consumers when pricing is often so out of sync with consumer demand. When prices increase for wines in other regions (usually marked up by the retailer or distributor) it’s because consumer demand outstrips production. When it comes to Bordeaux, it’s much harder to say. Those price increases could simply be because out-of-touch chateau owners thought they could get away with it. That’s the transparency that most consumers would want. I suspect if chateaus didn’t have the negociant system in place to provide automatic buyers, the pricing structures might be very, very different.

In many ways, I would argue Bordeaux is the most opaque region due to their reliance on the negociants and now also how much they hold back. Elsewhere, producers sell through the large majority of their production on a yearly basis, and yes, it’s fairly trivial to get production information on pretty much any major winery. I have no idea what the actual market supply is because so many of these chateau hold back such a sizable proportion. And more than any other region, there is oceans of bordeaux wine in negociant warehouses. There is always wine buildup for every region, but Bordeaux most of all due to its large production and the negociant system.

Bottom line, at least for me, I’m only willing to pay what it’s worth to me to drink, regardless of how many they held back or sold.

Same for the rest of us, which is why so few are currently buying.

posts.name: Brian Befano
posts.cooked:
I know a few old-timers that passed on 1982, thinking 82 Mouton was insane at $35. They purchased 1961 for about $10 or less. 82 was a massive increase over 1978.

Just for the sake of interest and forgiving the back-of-the-napkin Google math, $10 in 1961 translates to around $90 in today’s dollars. If 2020 MR is going to be $700, that means prices are roughly 7.8 times what they used to be, adjusted for inflation. I completely agree with previous comments about more wealth in the world (see more people + Bordeaux is a fixed plot of land), but it is interesting to note that median income in the US has only gone up 1.5 times, adjusted for inflation, and the world population has only gone up 2.5 times since '61.

People starting off today are at a distinct disadvantage compared to previous generations (from a US perspective at least) when trying to learn about or build a cellar with these fine wines. Business is business, so no judgement here, but this might be why younger US demographics (from my view) are largely anti-Bordeaux, anti-decades-long-aging-required-purchases, and are looking to things like natural wine and lesser heralded regions, which is in some ways changing the palate of future generations of wine drinkers.

This is true. But you also need to keep in mind that in those days, maybe, 2-3 vintages a decade were good to great. Meaning, less good wines to choose from. Also, in those days, after the very best estates, there was a massive drop in quality. Even many of the Second Growths, most of Pomerol, Saint Emilion and Pessac Leognan were making so-so wines in the majority of vintages.

Today, that is not the case.

Not only are vintages consistently better, but the majority of estates today are producing better wine than ever. You also need to consider that today, there are numerous 2nd, 3rd, 5th growths, etc., that are making better wine on a consistent basis than most of the First Growths were making, except in the best vintages during the 50s, '60s, '70s, '80s and '90s.

So, while it is much more money to buy the very top echelon of wines, you do not need to spend that much money to drink extraordinary world-class wine.

With that in mind, are today’s consumers worse off? Not in my opinion.

What do you guys think?

This has been my experience in hobby markets where quality can be improved. Increases in demand drive prices for the trophy products into nose bleed levels, but many more suppliers act on the incentives by producing higher quality products to fill the larger market. So yes, I don’t drink pappy as my daily, however there is simply way more good bourbon available, for cheaper, today then there was 15 years ago. I’ll take today’s market over the idealized pre-social media/internet markets all day long.

It is an interesting discussion, and one of the areas where you and I have differed over the years. The wines today are bigger, richer, riper, fatter, less precise and for me far less interesting than the wines from the 1980s etc. Not all of it can be attributed to global warming, but it certainly is a major factor. There is less of the crapshoot weather factor from mid September to August than you before.

But there are other factors at work, not least the definition of ripeness. Winemaking has changed, and cellar practices too. Whether it makes for better wines is a good question. To some extent I think it is self fulfilling; the new style became the norm, and critics like you love them and sell them hard. Luddites like myself wonder if we are the last generation who will taste the slightly herbal, barely ripe beauties that was the hallmark of Bordeaux.

Incidentally I would argue the golden decade in Bordeaux was from 1981-1990. Apart from some miserable vintages such as 1984 and 1987, it was a time when Bordeaux was arguably at its best.

posts.name: Ryan A.ns.el
posts.cooked:
I have not wanted to jump into this conversation but it seems many are focused on either first growth/expensive or bust (and most commenters here seem more traditionalists/classic preferences which is great but not all of us are… Some of the best wines are not the most expensive (yes I buy expensive wines so I am not a naysayer) and the vintages have their own impact. Examples - I prefer a more modern take on Bordeaux than most of you on WB, drinking window in general for me 6-25 years of course with exceptions.

These are my opinions only - The statement of backfilling 2019 vs 2020 to save the 10-30% may make sense for some but I would argue not for all as its not a linear price equation (just buy the cheapest) as there has to be some art in the decision. 2020 from what I have read between the two vintages may have more of a modern lean although not to the extent of 2018 which for many of you may not be in your wheelhouse but for those that prefer a touch more- it might be either (a) worth buying their favorites (2020 vintage) at the higher prices OR buying alternatives that are a touch more cost effective yet still with the personal 2020 vintage benefits. Lets be honest, the first growths are not always the best and therefore don’t always need to be purchased to get the ‘best wine’ as the ‘best’ wine for someone might not be the ‘best’ wine for someone else (vintages impact on the wine, desired timeline to drink, winemaking influence, varietal influence, etc etc). One example of way too many might be the preference on Merlot or Cab Franc or both vs predominant Cab. Since there are no First Growths where these are the major drivers, it is impossible for First Growths to be the best wine for you. Now you could argue Petrus, Le Pin, Cheval, Ausone, Angelus, Pavie etc etc are First Growths on the Right bank but are they? I would always put Vieux Chateau Certan (VCC) in any of those conversations and that wine is similar (quality, vineyard, winemaking team, et) relative to its peers - sooo if you normally buy the more expensive options, perhaps step down price wise in 2020 and buy VCC. This example is one of many and while you can’t of course get the same exact wine it should be similar enough to follow the same logic across Bordeaux (not suggesting if you buy Haut Brion you would get the same satisfaction from Clarence (obviously) but perhaps La Mission as those two trade at about 20% price discount (this is simply an illustration and not brought up to create a visceral reaction that its not exactly the same, profiles are different, etc etc - I am acknowledging all of this. Just keep in mind a HB in 2019 will taste different than in 2018 or 2020 so why not look for the same pricing discount in an alternative wine? In St Emilion instead of Valandraud buy Canon or Beausejour Duffau or Troplong (these days I am no longer sure which is more expensive but the same idea still holds true); if those are too expensive buy Beau Sejour Becot etc etc going all the way down to Tour St Christophre (~$25).

Again I know this will infuriate many that will argue the legitimacy of my simplistic analogies/illustrations are way off etc etc but the logic does hold true and is something to be considered if and when money at any price range in a particular vintage will impact your individual buying decision IMO or you can always reduce the quantity that is normally purchased in order to take advantage of a new vintage and its impact on your favorite wines.

82 was a massive increase over 1978.

2020 Canon is a better wine than 2019. And it will probably rise in value in the secondary market. When you consider that it competes with Angelus, Figeac, Pavie, etc., frankly, it is underpriced.

But it is a reasonable, forward-thinking way to lay down the better bottles, of which Canon is in that group.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
moving their wines to at least the 2019 secondary position on the market

But that’s the whole problem these days. The producers/distributors are marking things up to secondary market values so there’s no cheese there for the consumer. Whether it’s cult California wines, BDX EP or DRC, Coche and the like, you used to be able to get an allocation and drink some and sell some and come out pretty good. Or buy and hold and sell later at a nice return. Now the direct prices are more often in line with secondary market and it’s typically a losing proposition.

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked:
Ok

It’s 15.5% abv, so it’s gotta be better

Where did you see 15.5% ABV?

Full bottle 1,360 g. Cask sample taken 6 April. 80% Merlot, 20% Cabernet Sauvignon. Vines 39 years old.
Very dark right to the purplish rim. Rich and sumptuous aroma of pure, sweet, black fruit, entwined with oak’s sweet spice. Powerful and a little charry on the palate, the tannins thick and compact but already smooth. A big, alcoholic beast of a wine but ends with unexpected freshness, and, even with this high alcohol, just about in balance. Long, chewy finish. Wholesome, powerful. (JH) 15.5%
Drink 2030 – 2044

Julia Harding MW, JancisRobinson.com, April 2021

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Canon comes out tomorrow. Along with Troplong, Valandraud, and l’Eglise Clinet.

And I’m surprised no one has mentioned the eye-watering jump in Calon Segur - $119/btl up from $89 last year – a 33% increase.

Yeah, so much for “sneaky buy”. The 2020 price seemed so high I had to go back and see what I paid for the 2014 futures…$57!

It’s difficult to compare 2014 with 18/19 or 20. The vineyard was sold in 2012. Since then the new owners spent tens of millions replanting the vineyards, building new cellars etc.

All BDX was well priced in 2014. But Calon Segur in my opinion was artificially low as they were trying to build brand loyalty with the next generation of customers. Today, 2014 has doubled in price, so you know it was a good deal.

They want to compete with COS and Montrose and as far as quality goes, I think they’re at the same level today. And Calon is less money than both.

My guess is, within a decade, Calon will sell for about the same as Cos and Montrose.

I think the point about the price increase is that it defeats the point of EP, which isnt wrong except for the most highly allocated wines.

I didnt buy too much (bottles wise) in 2018, and this year will probably end up with about half of what I bought in 2019, owing to the price increases. When back vintages are both comparable, and available, it doesnt make a huge amount of sense.

My understanding of EP is that its an overhang from the 19th century when Chateau didnt have enough money to guarantee their ability to run, year on year, so needed to get cash in as quickly as possible. Where owners can now afford the running costs and dont mind keeping a bit of stock on hand, its obviously less of a concern, thus no need to discount too heavily EP

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked:
Buying EP continues to make sense for me, and even this year I feel i’ve found pockets of (good?) value.

So far, i’ve bought bottles:
Beausejour Becot: 6 + 1 mag
Larcis Ducasse: 6
Clos L’Eglise: 3
Pontet Canet: 1 + 1 mag
Feytit Clinet: 2

For most of these i’m confident that buying EP saves me 10-30%+ vs buying on release. I live in France and this has been my experience with 90% of what I’ve bought EP (11, 14, 15, 16, 19): nearly all have generated this order of savings and also allows to buy in <6 or 12 quantities, reduce shipping costs by all in one delivery, etc…

Now there is a range of Chateau (think Haut Marbuzet et al,) where you can find at EP price in supermarket autumn sales etc, so I try to avoid buying those EP.

This year, even the big increase on Pontet Canet still makes sense: 20 is released at same EP price as 2015, which is now north of €135, all in. So I look at EP as a mechanism to allow me to buy wines that in market are above what i’m comfortable spending on fermented grape juice!

Most of my purchasing is mainly around ‘Whats likely to get more expensive in the short term’, but I dont mind a little bit of arbitrage every now and then. Haven’t done enough to remotely make it a living, but its a nice little side earner.

Fact is, if someone is selling it, someone else is buying it, and I’m putting in the effort to match that buyer and seller, why shouldnt I make money from it?S eller had it listed at price X, buyer was offering price Y, so they’re both clearly happy with the prices on offer/being offered.

looking ahead, i think magnums will continue to command a premium, but probably less so. As more ‘investment’ money moves into wine, and EP allowing flexi-format ordering, I wouldnt be surprised if investors are buying more magnums/large formats anyway. Supply and demand from that side.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
My understanding of EP is that its an overhang from the 19th century when Chateau didnt have enough money to guarantee their ability to run, year on year, so needed to get cash in as quickly as possible.

EP as you know it is a fairly modern occurrence. Prior to 1983, very few consumers bought futures. Only a handful of merchants even offered the wines as futures.

Other than a few Right Bank wines, there were no allocations. There were no massive tastings for buyers, journalists etc.

The negociants bought the wines. But there was no rush to buy from them. Many of the top merchants didn’t even offer the wines until quite a while after they were delivered.

Robert Parker changed the game with the 1982 vintage.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Yep, at the end of the day, EP is a game that’s all about trying to guess in bottle scores.

In my experience on many of these wines lately, critics end up at the top end of their ranges, and there (feels like) there’s been a tendency with some of the major critics to score more conservatively EP. E.g. this year, between NM/AG there are very few potential 100 point scores (I think NM gave out none? I cant remember), Anson says she doesnt give 100 points EP (but if she did, 2019 Lafleur would be it). Jeff has scored quite generously this year…

You can see what I mean if you look at 2018 Canon here - Canon 2018. Fine Wine from Bordeaux

Not all, but many critics end up on the higher side of their estimates. (Note the dates, compare 2021 with 2019 obviously)

If we end up ‘high’, you’ll get 100 points from JL LPB, (realistically JS too lets be honest), 98 JD, 97 AG 98 NM.

farrvintners.com

Farr Vintners - Fine Wines

2015 Canon for example - 96 LPB, 100 NM, 100 JS 100 AG, 17.5 JR (lol), 98+ JD 18+ MJ.

You’d need it to perform better than hoped for in bottle to beat out most of the critics on 2015, but it’s definitely not in the wrong ballpark. AG gave 2015 96/98, for example, EP. NM was 98/100, in fairness.

well, two things - if wines were scored on a 5 star basis rather than a notionally (but not actually) 100 point scale, 4* to 5* would be “20% better”

At the end of the day, wine price is probably proportional to score ^2 (or cubed). Prices move incredibly quickly for incremental gains.

You can compare 2020 Canon to 2019 Canon, as is your right, but I’d instead compare it to the similarly scored St Emilions - and when compared to Cheval Blanc, Angelus, Belair Monange, Pavie, I’d argue it looks decidedly affordable.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Perhaps because I have more experience than most in this in this thread, the majority of the posts on this thread miss the point.

Of course, price is relevant. And it’s fun to guess the hit tickets. This same conversation has been taking place on the net since the 2005 for me, and for the remaining few who were on prodigy, since at least 2000.

But all the passion spent here seems solely focused on price. There is a lack of excitement about the quality, character and style of what that these wines might be like to have in your glass.

I’m curious. Much of the recent traffic in this thread is focused on Canon and Les Carmes Haut Brion. To get a solid baseline on what’s going on with those estates, has anyone tasted 2015 or 2016? If so, do you like them?

FWIW, 2020 is closer to 2015. Do you guys like these wines ?

edit: Just checking over, I have actually had 2012 and 2015 LCHB at a BBR tasting before. I seem to remember being quite happy with the wine, but no detail beyond that.

2012 and 2014 are not the same as 2015, 15 18, 19 or 20. If you do not recall 2015, or have not had 2016, you should taste a bottle.

The same for Canon. It is not the same wine as it was just a few years ago.

In fact, several wines people are talking about buying here are different. In most, and probably all cases, I prefer the wines being made today, but Figeac, La Conseillante, Troplong Mondot, Pontet Canet, Palmer and a host of other wines have shifted. It is important to know what these wines are like before laying them down for a decade or more.

The stylistic comparison to 2015 is very helpful Jeff. I have found that to be a relatively friendly vintage, rather like 1985 was back in the day. I am fine with that level of ripeness and as in 2015 will be buying proportionally more Margaux than I normally do.

2020 is lower in alcohol than 2015. But it is an opulent, sexy, silky, rich vintage. Texturally, I find the vintages share commonalities. If 2014 or 2012 are your favorite vintages, 2020 is not for you. If you like riper, silkier years, 2020 is going to be great.

It’s not only somewhat, but very expensive to try a recent
successful vintage of LCHB or Canon if you didn’t happen to be there for the EP. For example: Let’s say I bought 6 bottles of LCHB 2020 for 110€ incl tax in hopes to save some money by the time the wine matures. If I buy a 2016 as a test bottle, it’ll set me back a minimum of about 180€ which already represents a significant portion of my potential savings from the EP purchase. Besides that I already know it won’t be in its best drinking window when I open it.

So my planned strategy for a wine like this is: dump the whole case in the back of the cellar and forget it for 10-15 years. Then try a bottle and if I don’t like it, sell the rest and hopefully make a profit in the process.

Of course one is at the mercy of critics when choosing the wines, but isn’t that sort of a compliment to you? Besides, exactly predicting what one prefers to drink 10-15 years from now is kind of a crapshoot anyway as I see it.

I believe a rule on this board, anyone can correct me if I am wrong, when opening a just landed vintage Mag, you need to put the wine in airplane bottles and overnight to anyone on this board that shows an active interest in that wine.

My address is:
555 Send Wine
Any City, USA

Also please update your thoughts on near real time as I may hit refresh a couple (hundred) times waiting for your assessment. Also since you have not had any recent versions per your call out, please open ‘15/‘16 and use that as a comparisons.

Thank you from all of us on your commitment.

Thanks for bringing that up Jeff–the stylistic drift of Bordeaux is interesting, and when I think back on it, undeniable. I remember brand new 2005s being massively tannic to the point of unpleasantness, difficult (for my less experienced palate) to appreciate and to sense where the wine was heading. There is a world of difference between the 2005s and the recently arrived 2018s, which are delicious, balanced with smooth tannins, and significantly easier to appreciate the quality and complexity of the wine, especially after some air. It’s not a crime to drink these in their youth in my view. It’s probably unknowable, but do we think that the change in style could impact the wine’s aging? I have heard that the 1982s were famously open and drinkable from the beginning, and many didn’t shut down, so perhaps initial drinkability and future aging potential are not related qualities.

Matt… And if you are buying on my notes/advice, I appreciate your trust. And you are right, that is where the value of a critic, reviewer comes in. With a taster it is not as important what they score a wine, it is their ability or willingness to explain what you can expect. The style and character of a wine is more important than the sum of its points.

That being said, I doubt most people read the notes

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Thanks for bringing that up Jeff–the stylistic drift of Bordeaux is interesting, and when I think back on it, undeniable. I remember brand new 2005s being massively tannic to the point of unpleasantness, difficult (for my less experienced palate) to appreciate and to sense where the wine was heading. There is a world of difference between the 2005s and the recently arrived 2018s, which are delicious, balanced with smooth tannins, and significantly easier to appreciate the quality and complexity of the wine, especially after some air. It’s not a crime to drink these in their youth in my view. It’s probably unknowable, but do we think that the change in style could impact the wine’s aging? I have heard that the 1982s were famously open and drinkable from the beginning, and many didn’t shut down, so perhaps initial drinkability and future aging potential are not related qualities.

Good post! Terroir and the vintage makes the wine. 05 is a tannic, concentrated, long lived vintage. It’s structured and still youthful. Though I had about 10 different higher end wines this week and most were coming around.

This is a familiar pattern. I’m sure it was the same with 1929. Definitely in 59 and as you pointed out in 82. Folks stuck in the past felt the riper, silkier, sexier vintages would not age and evolve. They did!

And I’m betting that today’s riper, silkier years will also age and develop. 98, 00, 01, 05, 09 are all good vintages that are still young, vibrant and exciting.

That’s great, Jeff --appreciate the information and opinion . You inspired me to look up the 1929 and 1958 Bordeaux vintages, and of course your website is the first hit! Thank you for all you do for the community.

This for me was why I bought the 2020. It may not be ‘worth’ the price over the 2019, but I already have the 2019, I value some variety, and in comparison to other 2020 the Canon seems like a value.

Trying to understand the pricing of Cos d’Estournel… A quick look at wine-searcher tells you that it’s possible to march into a shop in UK today and buy a bottle of Cos 2005 for £149 tax incl. The vintage should be one of all time greats and the wine received excellent scores. All auction results for 2005 show similar prices. Is there really potential for the 2020 vintage to appreciate from the EP asking price? Most of the main critics aren’t rating this wine super high.

I’m skeptical on the price increase from EP as well, but it’s worth noting that they’re very different wines. The wines from 2000-2012 are modern to ultra-modern wines, and controversial to some. Having said that, the 2001 and 2002 are pretty nice to me - perhaps before Prats got really going with the modernization. Aymeric de Gironde took the estate in a completely different direction in 2014 - and it’s now back to being a much more “classical” or perhaps “post-modern” wine - the 2020 is just 13.5% alcohol.

i think that’s exactly correct. Two good vintages of Bordeaux at a 20-30% discount to ‘fair’ value is a precedent. And that starts to change the market structure.

By pricing where they have, all the 2019 stock will be cleared up - no matter how well priced people perceived it to be at that poin in time, itll get bought up now. And then suddenly you have 2020 at a ‘bit’ of a discount to 2018 (typically, but not across the board), at which point the price structure is back in line with ‘historic’

posts.name: Henry Brown

so, on today’s subject, our last challenge wine has been released.

d’Issan @ £42.17 per bottle (49 euros a bottle before tax)
Carmes Haut Brion @ £79 per bottle (91 euros a bottle before tax)
Margaux @ £433 per bottle (504 euros/bottle)
Clinet @ £66.67 per bottle (77 euros)
Pichon Baron @ £110.5 per bottle (129 euros before tax)


To win the bet, on the 1st of August 2031, these wines must add up to a total price of no less than £804.47 to buy a single bottle of each. The spirit of the bet is about appreciation of the underlying asset, so if there are some weird FX things in these 10 years, we’ll re-price to Euros or just be sensible and agree what the outcome is.

Bearing in mind Carmes Haut Brion is now trading circa £113.33 per bottle, this means we’re now at £765.67 for the parcel, so only £40 to go (and retain) which I believe Chateau Margaux will easily do. From my side I slightly regret the inclusion of clinet vs canon, but I think its still incredible QPR if you look at the other pomerols there. Pichon and d’Issan were Mark’s choice, they wouldn’t have been wines I included personally.

The wager, to remind, is a 2015 Pichon Lalande, which is a wine I’ve never had and certainly look forward to enjoying one of Mark’s bottles, with any luck

posts.name: M Matthews
posts.cooked:
Bordeaux wineries and negotiants are masters of supply and demand. 2019 was a fluke, not a market reset. Demand is managed through tranches, second (and third) wines, special labels all which will create scarcity.

Hard to appreciate what cost curve on these wines will be.

I’m interested to see what happens to 2017 pricing, might be a buyer if those wines go on sale. Even pricing on 2016 doesn’t look terrible given 2020 prices.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
But what do the professionals think about this? I read Jane Anson was not particularly happy either as commented on her Twitter. And also how do others see the special label gimmick? From an investment perspective it’s probably a good purchase, but is this a healthy phenomenon? Cos D’estournel 2020 was in general inferior than last year, but part of the price increase is due to the label.

What makes you think that when you have better vintages of many of these wines available which are around the same price… when the majority of wines of the better vintages of 2016/2018 haven’t had a good enough performance since EP to bring you any investment gain (just deduct the 20% the resellers takes from you away and you’re in many cases below the EP prices). Yes, in 10 years time and in the long run, there might be an investment performance (probably not even if 7 of the next 9 vintages are again great vintages) but at least short term I don’t see them perform particularly well. More likely than not, a lot of these 2020s will be available at the same price when they arrive physically in two years.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Bordeaux wineries and negotiants are masters of supply and demand. 2019 was a fluke, not a market reset. Demand is managed through tranches, second (and third) wines, special labels all which will create scarcity.

Other than the First Growths, chateaux do not release in tranches. They place on offer all they wish to sell. For appearance’s sake, they might release minor amounts to get press, but that is not the market.

Some negociants hold a portion of their stock back, gambling prices will rise in the secondary marketplace, which helps offset vintages like 2013, 2017, etc, others sell all they have to offer.

As to second and third wines, how do you conclude those are to create scarcity? That’s just silly.

posts.cooked:
Pay the extra money for the 2015, 2016, 2018, 2019 and 2020. In the long run, those will all be better wines and when you get around to opening them, you will not care that you paid more for those vintages.

I get this argument, as it is sometimes better to not buy a lesser product at all than it is to buy a poor version of something, but I still think “you will not care that you paid more” is oversimplifying the decision-making process a great deal. Sure, I would not care what I paid for my new (theoretical) Bugatti Veyron while I’m driving it around the track, but I very much might care or regret it when I’m not driving. And there is the possibility still that I hate it even when I’m driving.

I doubt many on this forum are deciding between paying the rent and a case of LCHB, but there are a lot of different reasons why a 50% or greater savings on a wine (compared to other years) in an “off” year might be a good buy. You gave it a 95! An $80, 95-rated Bordeaux should, in theory, be a great way to see (much faster) if you like the winemaker’s style and decide if you want to invest more in better years (or if you like this type of off-year profile a lot).

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Pay the extra money for the 2015, 2016, 2018, 2019 and 2020. In the long run, those will all be better wines and when you get around to opening them, you will not care that you paid more for those vintages.

I get this argument, as it is sometimes better to not buy a lesser product at all than it is to buy a poor version of something, but I still think “you will not care that you paid more” is oversimplifying the decision-making process a great deal. Sure, I would not care what I paid for my new (theoretical) Bugatti Veyron while I’m driving it around the track, but I very much might care or regret it when I’m not driving. And there is the possibility still that I hate it even when I’m driving.

I doubt many on this forum are deciding between paying the rent and a case of LCHB, but there are a lot of different reasons why a 50% or greater savings on a wine (compared to other years) in an “off” year might be a good buy. You gave it a 95! An $80, 95-rated Bordeaux should, in theory, be a great way to see (much faster) if you like the winemaker’s style and decide if you want to invest more in better years (or if you like this type of off-year profile a lot).

You cannot compare a Bugatti Veyron, which starts at about $2,000,000 and goes up to $15,000,000 or so with a bottle of wine. That is absurd.

There are 2017 wines that offer value. Especially the whites and of course some reds. There are always exceptions to every rule. But for the vast majority of wines that are going to be cellared and enjoyed 10,15-20 years from now, consumers are better off buying the top vintages. Those will not only offer better tasting experiences, but they will also be more fun to bring to the table when they are eventually opened.

There are some wine buyers that do not like what I call the better vintages as a matter of style. I get that. But the majority of buyers are going to be happier having purchased 15, 16, 18, 19 and 20 over 2017.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
The 2017s are not really worth it as there are so many other great vintages. They just don‘t have the depth to truly excite today and in the long run. I would rather buy less of the good vintages (in a fixed budget situation).

I think any vintage ending with a 7 is cursed. The last great vintage in Bordeaux with a 7 was 1947, and the best of the 7s after that may be 2017.

2017 is not a bad vintage, and if it were 40% below the others mentioned, it would be a perfectly acceptable purchase. The wines are pretty, nicely balanced and is likely to make for nice early drinking. They may not have the intensity of other recent ones, but you are not going to have to wait twenty five years to drink them at their best. But the Bordelais can be a little greedy, and it’s not priced by the guys who will have huge number of unsold cases lying in inventory, it’s been the chateaux who have been calling the shots. They do have the upper hand in Bordeaux, so you have idiotic pricing decisions with very little consequence, such as with 2017.

Perhaps I mistook your advice on Canon, but I thought the recommendation was that it is important to try wines from different vintages before you commit to buying them in any reasonable quantity. The huge discount on '17 LCHB that was described vs. other vintages seems like a great opportunity to learn about a wine and develop preferences in a more near-term timeline. At a 95 rating, the '17 is a 38% discount over the current price of the '20 (which you rated a 97). If the question is buying the best wine, I don’t think people need advice to just pay whatever for wine with the highest rating. Maybe I’m just a miser, though.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Completely removing how much you pay from your decision to buy something is not helpful, IMO.

I think in this case the point is that when you have that wine in the cellar in 20 years ready to open a couple of things are going to happen.

  • We’re talking about small differences. If you paid 20%, 30% or even 50% more or less, it’s a small enough difference that it’s not going to significantly change your perception of the wine at that time. $100 vs $150 or $500 vs $600 isn’t really that materially different. On the other hand if you paid $1000 instead of $100, that’s a large enough delta that does change your perception of the bottles and you will likely be more selective in opening and enjoying the 10x bottle, not to mention potentially having 10x as many bottles, so that’s a completely different conversation.


  • Whatever you paid now is going to feel like much less at that time due to the inevitable price inflation, thereby making the delta feel even smaller. It’s also likely that 2040 BDX prices will far outweigh 2018 or 2020 prices, making whatever price you paid now feel cheap. Is anyone complaining they bought 82 Mouton at $35 when they could have backfilled 78 Mouton for $15?

Therefore, the argument goes, buy the wine you like rather than pinching pennies with 2018 vs 2019 vs 2020 that are all within fairly reasonable ranges of each other. For the most part it makes sense. Now if you’re in the hobby or business of flipping, 20% or 30% is huge, even 10% is a big enough reason to buy one or the other.

Bottom line, buy the wines that make you happy at prices you want to drink them at.

they haave plenty of Cos left, they arent even allocating Lafite (and yesterday sent a rather desperate looking email to highlight the fact that they still had stock), 30+ cases of Pontet, 14 cases of LLC left, no allocations on LMHB or HB,


I’ve been tracking BBR on 2020 Cos - Buy 2020 Cos d'Estournel, St Estèphe, Bordeaux Wine - Berry Bros. & Rudd - they startedd with 50 x 6x75, now down to 33.



This is absolutely not a sell out EP season, a few top tier wines that are either extremely good this vintage (e.g. Canon, Ausone, Larcis), or pricing close/above their 2019 secondary absolutely will sell out, but I’d be surprised if you cant buy HB, LMHB, Angelus, Pavie, etc, all after EP at the same price as they were offered. Perhaps even less, if merchants need to clear stock to free capital. I’ve heard rumours of some secret discounts on top tier wines but I cant go into any more detail on that unfortunately.

Infact, the one thing this season has caused to sell out, is 2019. As another poster in here mentioned, its kind of funny seeing the wines that werent considered good bargins in 2019 getting snapped up now

I fully expect Mouton to come out about or above the Margaux pricing. FRW forecast up to £2600-2800/6, which is a ridiculous jump from £1796/6 last year (albeit secondary puts them somewhere between 2100-2400/6 right now), considering by all accounts 2019 was a better year for them. I dont think they can breach the £3k barrier, simply because Lafite kinda puts a cap?

posts.name: Ryan A.ns.el
posts.cooked: 2019 was COVID therefore unlike other years travel to Bordeaux to barrel sample was fairly straightforward. Since COVID shut everything down, the window to taste shrank causing limited availability. In addition barrel samples that were flown out last year were haphazard as it was the first time having to mobilize to that size- and not all samples arrived as one would need to make an informed note. Even today, WS as an example hasn’t reviewed any of the wines from 2019 or 2020 and will not do so until he is in Bordeaux. Fast forward, there is limited reason for the chateaus to push additional 2019 barrel samples outside of logistics alone- why not wait until bottles can be shipped within the net 8 months and perhaps have better stability and the corresponding scores.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
2019 was COVID therefore unlike other years travel to Bordeaux to barrel sample was fairly straightforward. Since COVID shut everything down, the window to taste shrank causing limited availability. In addition barrel samples that were flown out last year were haphazard as it was the first time having to mobilize to that size- and not all samples arrived as one would need to make an informed note.

Even today, WS as an example hasn’t reviewed any of the wines from 2019 or 2020 and will not do so until he is in Bordeaux. Fast forward, there is limited reason for the chateaus to push additional 2019 barrel samples outside of logistics alone- why not wait until bottles can be shipped within the net 8 months and perhaps have better stability and the corresponding scores.

Not exactly. The wines the OP was asking about were not sent to anyone in 2019. They did send samples in 2020.

WS made a choice in advance not to score barrel samples as an editorial policy.

Interestingly, 2019 was better for me, and as a guess to the others in America for receiving samples. The 2020 vintage was hung up in customs more often for some unknown reason. I hear other USA tasters had similar issues.

One of my shipments that contained 240 bottles seems to lost 120 bottles! They never arrived. The wines had to be replaced. I think in England, due to Brexit, it was not always easy receiving samples, but I have no idea.

All things considered, it’s amazing that 900 samples arrived in good condition for tasting.

FWIW, 2020 was more hectic, wines arrived fast and furious. 2019, because it was the first time sending samples arrived at a relaxed pace. It was easier to schedule Zoom tastings. 2020 was a rocket ship.

Regardless, it was a once-in-a-lifetime experience that I won’t forget.

I think it was amazing that you got so many good samples. I have done Primeur tasting in Bordeaux when samples were collected a day or two before and were sub par when they arrived at the tasting rooms. Do you know if they used gas to keep the samples intact?

All the samples were fedexed or flown directly to LA and delivered within 2-5 days of being pulled and tasted. Everything arrived cool to the touch. The chateaux I asked said they didn’t use gas.

In full-disclosure, I asked maybe 10-12 chateaux to resend as I know their wines and we discussed my impressions and both of us knew the samples were off or tired. The replacements were overnighted promptly.

As an aside, I found an extra sample from 2019 that had fallen into a box in the cellar. It was light, and not as complex as it was, but it was clearly drinkable even though it was 1 year old!

I think it was amazing that you got so many good samples. I have done Primeur tasting in Bordeaux when samples were collected a day or two before and were sub par when they arrived at the tasting rooms. Do you know if they used gas to keep the samples intact?

All the samples were fedexed or flown directly to LA and delivered within 2-5 days of being pulled and tasted. Everything arrived cool to the touch. The chateaux I asked said they didn’t use gas.

In full-disclosure, I asked maybe 10-12 chateaux to resend as I know their wines and we discussed my impressions and both of us knew the samples were off or tired. The replacements were overnighted promptly.

As an aside, I found an extra sample from 2019 that had fallen into a box in the cellar. It was light, and not as complex as it was, but it was clearly drinkable even though it was 1 year old!

What do you do with the rest of the bottles after you sample - it’s like 10 cases of wine all open at the same time

Generally speaking, because I had Zoom tasting sessions first thing in the morning, we tasted between 35-50 wines 5 days a week.

I had a team of volunteer sommeliers that rotated on a daily basis to help. They took home as many bottles to share with friends as they liked. Then friends, neighbors and family took home bottles as well.

The best bottles were usually tasted throughout the day or at a dinner with friends. It was an incredible experience for all of us.

The same process was used with barrel samples and bottled wines.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: In 1996 I came to Bordeaux in June to taste primeur 1995. Well over 100 wines from the UGC. As we were in a chateau, we mixed the left over wine and put them into a barrel. We called it Macarbre after our first named Mark, Charles and Bruno. Sadly the wine was spoiled. I tried a bottle a couple of years later, and it smelled as if a skunk had peed into the vat.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
d’Issan @ £42.17 per bottle (49 euros a bottle before tax)
Carmes Haut Brion @ £79 per bottle (91 euros a bottle before tax)
Margaux @ £433 per bottle (504 euros/bottle)
Clinet @ £66.67 per bottle (77 euros)
Pichon Baron @ £110.5 per bottle (129 euros before tax)

So as of right now, Farr are at about £2900/6 on Margaux = £483.33/bottle, and Carmes is £720/6 = £120/bottle

This means technically the 5 selected wines are a total of 12.5% up - but there is still some (uncertain) stock of Margaux at the original release price. It’ll take until in bottle most likely to clear up, based on my experience of trying to snipe some cheap 2019 EP before 2020 kicked off.

Similarly, there’s also claimed stock on the Carmes at first tranche, but both UK offers are ‘allocation only, please enquire’.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Ouch, but I have heard stories of negotiants saying they got small tranches and still can’t sell them, the inference being that there may be problems when the wine is finally released.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
yep, thats why I’m definitely not declaring victory yet. The question is really about when these wines hit bottle and the floodgates on stock get released to the market. We’ve got a potentially poor 2021 to negotiate, as well, and who knows what 2022 will look like. Plus, up-scoring or down-scoring in the bottles will be critical as well - the last few vintages have resulted in wines that score at the top end of their en primeur scores (in general), but whose to say that will apply for 2020 as well. On our pick of 5, one key weakness is that only one is right bank, and the general consensus is that merlot and cab franc did very well this year.

It’s a good start, but a very long road with many key obstacles before we’ll have any clear indication IMHO.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: What has historically happened to Bordeaux prices during global recessions? I didn’t really get in to wine until post 2008.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: 2008 dipped with the financial crisis and represents one of the best EP returns you’d make in recent history, despite not being an epic drinking vintage AFAIK. It dripped in the early 10s as well both becaause of the Bordeaux bubble popping but some technical recessions there. Obviously last year is clear.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
2008 dipped with the financial crisis and represents one of the best EP returns you’d make in recent history, despite not being an epic drinking vintage AFAIK. It dripped in the early 10s as well both becaause of the Bordeaux bubble popping but some technical recessions there. Obviously last year is clear.

Right - I get that it impacted EP… I guess I’m curious to know the broader impact. Like, what happened with the prices of say 1989 HB or 2000 Margaux during the 2008-2010 recession. Did they drop, and how long did they take to recover?

W-S doesn’t go back that far, but you can see the 3 month dip in 1989 HB prices, which has since risen 15% over its pre-pandemic high. Not quite S&P performance but it tracks more to the stock market than economic output. This was a very different recession than the last, clearly. The next recession, if accompanied by higher inflation, may be a bit more of a doozy.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: I cant speak to previous years, but if we look at 2020, I’d guess you see a real price cut of about 20%, which bounced fairly quickly. I was getting sent offers for “below market” high end Burgundy, Bordeaux, etc. Hard to be sure if its a private collector offloading to raise capital, or restaurants.

In that price range I was only able to taste one 2020 but I provided a few others, a little higher and a little lower.

2018 Enclos Tourville was a surprise and the team has continued to invest in the quality (new owner started investing in 2017) so I will assume the 2020 is excellent (I tried to increase my purchase after tasting but my distributor was sold out).

Pichon Lalande Comtesse (priced higher but they have really stepped it up since 2018 (tasted 2018 & 2020). I also thought they did a bang up job on their reserve in 2020 (more than half the price of the wines you mentioned).

La Gravette de Certan (VCC 2nd label and 30% lower cost than your list) - grab the 2020 when it comes out as it doesn’t feel like a 2nd wine it was that good.

I obviously have others I would recommend (and not from the trip based on vertical tasting progression, investment, etc but I didn’t personally taste other 2020s in this range that I loved like mentioned above.

Cheers

One critic or taster can be wrong but when you put all of them together, you usually get to a consensus which in the past few years turn out to be quite accurate in indentifying the true stars of the vintage. Then put your own grid of preferences over that consensus top list and you get a good buying list. In 2020 it’s a bit more difficult as not all critics rated all wines (Covid) but still here are some insights on this top list for 2020. The best wines are out of your price range (Lafleur, Margaux, Ausone, Haut Brion, Mouton, Cheval). In your price category-ish are the following wines (the upper half is mostly in the 130-180 category, the lower part is more around 100 with some below that):

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
I was in Bordeaux for a three days of visits in late October, and got to taste a few 2020s (most chateau were pouring 2018 or 2019), but the minuscule sample of 2020’s I tried were impressive*. It is a big, deep, structured vintage with lots of concentration. The chateau were clearly excited about the year. My immediate (shallow, biased, unhelpful) impression was the best of these will take a long time to come around.

*I was blown away by the barrel sample of the 2020 VCC (the price is equally astounding). Probably the best new or even young wine I’ve ever tried.

I find Montrose to be a muscular, stony, structured wine, especially when young. It’s cerebral and precise, not hedonistic or silky like Poyferre tends to be. The 96 Montrose still isn’t ready. And I have not enjoyed Malescot recently - the last two I’ve had (2004 & 2017) have both been unbearably glossy and slicked over with wood.

I agree they’re different. I like Poyferre, which I find modern but elegant and full of personality. Malescot shows much more oak and opulence - way too much for me. They’re both on the softer and flashier end of the spectrum though – the opposite of Montrose.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
I find Montrose to be a muscular, stony, structured wine, especially when young. It’s cerebral and precise, not hedonistic or silky like Poyferre tends to be.

Historically, yes, but Montrose is a lot more polished these days! Not as fleshy or dramatic as Poyferré, sure, but no longer chalk and cheese.

Definitely things have changed here. I’ve recently tried the 2016 blind, and all of us got it completely wrong as we couldn’t believe how polished, round, feminine this wine is.

My TN: Bombastic nose with layers and layers of expressive aromas of dark berries, bright red fruit, crushed rocks, fresh and roasted herbs and some Mouton-like burnt sugar aromas, I‘ve never experienced in a Montrose before. A 98/99 pts nose, just wow, wow, wow. On the palate the wine is spectacular too with super precise red and dark fruit, burnt sugar, roasted aromas, minerality, herbs all embedded in a superb structural frame with fine tannins and a perfect structure. The wine is light and airy but not in the same category yet as the Ausone in the flight before. Long, expanding and complex finish. What was surprising, and the same is true for the Cos, is how open, ready and feminine these St. Estephe wines were showing. This is without doubt the most spectacular young Montrose I‘ve ever had and I‘m pretty sure this can reach perfection. So far, this is one of the wines of the vintage for me. 97/98 pts in the context of this tasting with upside for more.

posts.name: Charles Fuss
posts.cooked:
I dont agree. I know people who are buying a lot of 2019 back stock because of 2020 pricing. If 2019 is likely to be the cheapest in +/- 5 years, guess what everyone is gonna buy - 2019. Esp when its at least comparable to 2020. Farr have sold 36 bottles of 2019 Chevalier rouge in the last 12 hours alone. Lack of supply at one price point intrinsically means price increase!

K&L has already re-stocked on 2019 Leoville Barton Pre-Arrivals…and those sold out at higher prices as well…! ($90 last restock vs initial first tranche $75 I believe). I recently purchased 2019 Leoville Barton from Laguna Cellar at $75, so they can still be found from smaller importers/retailers who still have allocations from earlier tranches to sell - but those will sell through soon enough. It’s possible that if/when K&L obtains more allocation for 2019 Leoville Barton it could be the same price as the 2020 ($95).

Point being - we’re already seeing a huge advantage in 2019 for early tranches of EP. Not even a year later and we’ve seen that discount start to disappear relative to 2020 pricing. Some Forex could be in play - but the boat is likely going to have “sailed” here within the next few months I bet. Chateaux will undoubtedly increase prices 20-30%+ on later releases of 2019, especially if these are rated higher in bottle.

Despite all the discussion on this board through the last decade, my experience is that if you find a wine compelling, and enough of a value during early tranches of EP, buy it if you can afford it. You’ll either lack access to the same wine later on, and if it is accessible, it will likely come at a premium to EP. If you’re really lucky, you may be able to snap it up for 5-10% over initial EP, but most of the time it comes at 15%-30%-45% increase. It’s just like the stock market - something is too expensive so they don’t buy it, then it goes up in price over time, and they snap it up during a dip down the road thinking they got a good value…but the reality is they still bought well over the price of their first opportunity to buy.

William Kelley on the 2019 Palmer
The 2019 Palmer is a rich, dramatic wine that soars from the glass with aromas of cherries, blackberries and berry fruit liqueur mingled with notions of violets, rose petals and sweet spices. Full-bodied, layered and seamless, it’s powerful and immensely concentrated […]

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That‘s laughable… Palmer better than Margaux? In which universe…

The universe we inhabited in the 60s and 70s, maybe.

It seems strange for Gilman to say that too. My impression was that Palmer has been moving in a direction that Gilman would like less (more extracted and higher alcohol than Ch. Margaux).

Maybe decades ago, that might be true. But Gilman commented on a 2020 tasting and comment and in recent years there are lightyears between Palmer and Margaux. And agree, I found the most recent vintages of Palmer all quite Californian (for a left bank), more so than any other high profile left bank Bordeaux.

William Kelley on the 2019 Palmer
The 2019 Palmer is a rich, dramatic wine that soars from the glass with aromas of cherries, blackberries and berry fruit liqueur mingled with notions of violets, rose petals and sweet spices. Full-bodied, layered and seamless, it’s powerful and immensely concentrated […]

I wouldn’t say Californian per se, but rather that they show all the flesh and richness that you’d expect from a Merlot-dominated blend, cropped at low yields, in very warm vintages. Thomas Duroux embraces what nature gives him and does a great job with it, but the result is obviously rather larger-scaled, more textural wines.

Anyone who is nostalgic for a more classically-proportioned Palmer will want to look at the 2021, which is terrific.

d’Issan @ £42.17 per bottle (49 euros a bottle before tax)

To win the bet, on the 1st of August 2031, these wines must add up to a total price of no less than £804.47 to buy a single bottle of each. From my side I slightly regret the inclusion of clinet vs canon, but I think its still incredible QPR if you look at the other pomerols there. Pichon and d’Issan were Mark’s choice, they wouldn’t have been wines I included personally.

d’Issan has remain basically unchanged at £41.67/bottle
Carmes Haut Brion is now at £112.50/bottle
Margaux is £483/bottle
Clinet at £66.33/bottle
Pichon Baron trading at £110/bottle

So the aggregate cost here is £813.50 - which means I’m apparnetly home dry on the prices for the win right now, I just need the prices to sustain over the next 9 years. Given the stock market of late, I dont think that has impacted these yet, and clearly we need to see how they start to taste in bottle and actually move on the market when physical before I get remotely celebratory.

It shows for me the strength of the wine market, that overpriced as they are, 2021 is still generating serious interest.

That being said, I did stock up on 2019, but ended up buying only two estate’s 2020, Cheval and VCC 2020.
And as my theory is that whether or not they sell 2021, it sets up yet higher pricing for 2022 and 2023.

They better be careful, or else they run the risk of quickly losing whatever customers they’ve gained back since the release of the '14s (that’s when I jumped back in, after having been mostly “out” over the previous 8 to 10 years). Most Bordeaux is made in quantities too large for them to be considered inelastic goods — there’s a whole world of fine, fun, and interesting wine out there — people don’t need Bordeaux!

The 2021s really don’t matter. Whether it sells or not, it is a small vintage, and many estates are holding back a significant percentage so as not to put too much pressure on the negociants.

Meanwhile, savvy buyers are scurrying to buy older vintages that look good in comparison to the 2021s, a sort of market enema. It will clean out a lot of current inventory and create a sort of equilibrium.


I think much of the consumer anger, a few years ago, came after the Chinese entered the market. The trade got excited, it was a new toy, and it was Chinese this and Chinese that. The American market got short shift, as the marketing here became perfunctory at best, and chateau visits were reduced to large events. Some loyal Bordeauxphiles felt disrespected. This is just a pricing question, so it does not feel as personal.

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
The universe we inhabited in the 60s and 70s, maybe.

It seems strange for Gilman to say that too. My impression was that Palmer has been moving in a direction that Gilman would like less (more extracted and higher alcohol than Ch. Margaux).

I just read WK’s note on 2019 Ch. Margaux and it’s difficult to square with the topic.

Maybe decades ago, that might be true. But Gilman commented on a 2020 tasting and comment and in recent years there are lightyears between Palmer and Margaux. And agree, I found the most recent vintages of Palmer all quite Californian (for a left bank), more so than any other high profile left bank Bordeaux.

William Kelley on the 2019 Palmer
The 2019 Palmer is a rich, dramatic wine that soars from the glass with aromas of cherries, blackberries and berry fruit liqueur mingled with notions of violets, rose petals and sweet spices. Full-bodied, layered and seamless, it’s powerful and immensely concentrated […]

I wouldn’t say Californian per se, but rather that they show all the flesh and richness that you’d expect from a Merlot-dominated blend, cropped at low yields, in very warm vintages. Thomas Duroux embraces what nature gives him and does a great job with it, but the result is obviously rather larger-scaled, more textural wines.

Anyone who is nostalgic for a more classically-proportioned Palmer will want to look at the 2021, which is terrific.

I agree with Gilman on this one. I have not discussed the topic of Margaux vs. Palmer with him, but I suspect our reasonings are along similar lines.

Looking back at all the changes in Bordeaux since the mid 90s and where things stand today, I think Mouton and Palmer have in recent vintages finally hit on the magic forumla after a lot of experimentation- that synthesis of traditional and modern whereby you get a really balanced and terroir-driven ageworthy wine that also can be enjoyed much younger than would normally be the case.

There are of course some others where tradition has held and changes were minor, if any (Lafite, Latour and Montrose for example) - but for those that opted for dramatic changes, Mouton and Palmer have been the first to arrive at a formula that delivers absolutely breathtaking wines that can appeal to traditionalist and modernist alike.

The trouble with Chateau Margaux, IMHO, is that the wines are too extracted and oaky at the expense of the inherent delicacy and finess of what makes that wine great. Delicacy and finesse may be at odds with sheer volume- but they are not at odds with the concepts of persistence, succulence or longevity. There is a way to find that balance between old and new, but it has not happened yet.

With Palmer and Mouton- yes the wines are jammier and bigger, but they have a great balance of elements and in terms of terroir expression they are actually more digitally defined on a consistent basis than in any prior decade where I have multiple tasting notes. One of the great things about 1986 Mouton, and a big reason why I prefer it to the 1982, is the brilliant precision of its subtle details. Now that brilliance is more frequently evident- and from day one.

I would say the exact same of Palmer. Bigger and jammier - yes, but with the terroir magnificently presented. I would rather have the 2014 over just about any vintages of the 90s or 00s.

Margaux just has not gotten there. The 1996 is a great wine- I gave it 5 stars- but even there I really wish the oak had been a bit less heavy. The 2012 is very attractive in its way, but is not going to age well and a lost opportunity to make something lighter and more elegant in the classic Margaux style. The 1999 started off with great promise, but got to be a bit too jammy with time- covering up all those gorgeous aromatics that were present at release. They are making good wine- sometimes even great- but not compelling wine. And at that price and with that vineyard potential- compelling is a reasonable expectation.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I began to find Margaux problematic after the brilliant 1983. We did a blind 1985 tasting and Margaux was clearly the worst wine there. The 1986 tasted blind against the Rauzan Segla did not show well, and the Rauzan was unanimously voted on as better. And so it continued, and after couple of times Margaux verticals, I became increasingly skeptical. Exceptions 1996 and 2005, but otherwise, very second tier.

Palmer on the other hand, has been making brilliant wines in the same period. I stopped tasting annually in 2016, but have tasted the massive 2018, and given the yields I am not sure whether it is my kind of wine. It is clearly going to be great, but it will certainly be controversial.

But the 2005 and 2010 are both destined to be great, great Palmers. And great, great Palmers are in the elite of Bordeaux.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Fantastic posts, guys! Great reads on Margaux, Mouton and Palmer.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Needless to say, I do not agree with you on Margaux. Today, I think the wines are better than ever. They are silkier and display incredible levels of purity. They have reduced yields and increased selection dramatically, so these days, a much larger percentage of the harvest is placed into the second, third, and even a 4th wine. The new cellars help with parcel by parcel vinification. Today, I find the wines to be extraordinary.

Palmer is also making the best wines in the history of the estate! They are everything great Bordeaux should be. I taste every new vintage in barrel and in bottle as well as older vintages too and that’s my take. YMMV

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
One of the head turners for me at the 2020 UGC was the Le Gay Pomerol. A very exotic, sexy bouquet, which the proprietor indicated was imparted, as least in part, by using 100% new oak (it didn’t seem like oak to me, not at least until he told what I was smelling).

I’m not usually a big fan of lavish amounts of new oak, but at least on this night it was very attractive.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked: Meanwhile, the 2020 Canon and Figeac did little to quicken the pulse.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: For those interested, Jeff has started publishing his 2020 notes.

posts.name: Demian Saenz
posts.cooked: Total Wine just charged me for the other half of my 2020 Chateau Margaux order. My 2019 Margaux was just delivered this past August.

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l

posts.name: Philip Bennett
