posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked: I’ve heard from two distributors, one from NY and one from NorCal, that much of 2020 won’t be coming in until July.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: William Kelley’s (as ever) outstanding report on the wines in bottle is out today. I’m still combing through it. Lots of interesting nuances – there are many wines where he has different opinions than those of the critics I’ve seen so far. If you aren’t subscribing, I believe it’s worth it to sign up.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
Thanks Glen. So, reflecting on WK’s very lucid notes - it seems like WK thought several properties made their best wines in recent memory, but many more stalwarts had less successful showings than in 2019. Some of the usual contenders for WOTV he found significantly less compelling than 2019 include PLL, Ausone, Trotanoy, and Cos. And he has some pretty direct criticism of Pontet-Canet, which he didn’t like in 2019 either. Sounds like 2020 will generally require more patience than 2019.

Due to high pricing, I bought a very limited selection of 2020s EP, but very happy I picked up Canon for $139. I was on the fence and didn’t buy Figeac and Montrose, which seems to have been the right call. They’re still the same price as at EP but maybe not for a lot longer with the WK100s.

posts.name: Marco Fimiani
posts.cooked: I found his notes on Cos interesting when compared to the notes of some other wine writers. From some other reviews, I was of the mind that it would be following up on much of the success/direction of the 2019. However, WK’s note suggests otherwise. I’ll need to review further and wonder whether the timing of tastings - that WK openly criticizes in his article - had a hand.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Fantastic report @William_Kelley !

Not only are abv levels included in the notes but there is so much commentary on the style and that is also reflected in the scores (Pontet, Cos have been mentioned here). That clear style preference is so incredibly helpful and makes 100pts for Conseillante, Figeac, Canon or 98pts for Rauzan Segla much stronger and more informative. Congrats, William, keep up the good work and the boldness.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
To me, it was definitely richer, flatter and higher pH (3.9 vs 3.7 on a logarithmic scale) than the 2019. Not sure if that is just a question of having a lot of Merlot in a vintage where harvest date was critical (and Cos certainly are picking a bit earlier than they used to), or uneasiness with the stylistic swing towards fresher, more elegant, less syrupy/creamy wines.

When I write notes like these I simply try to communicate what I would if one of you on this forum asked me what I thought. It is too easy to get sucked into worrying “what will the château think?” and I tend to think that self-censorship is one of the banes of contemporary wine criticism.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I don’t include them systematically but I do try to put them in when it contributes to the sense of the note. Similarly with blends, pHs, IPTs etc… They shouldn’t be there as filler but in some cases, when properly explained and contextualized, they are interesting and meaningful information.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: In general tasting early did a disservice to the wines. But in the case of some blowsy/flat/high abv wines, tasting them just after bottling when they were a bit tighter might have actually seen them show better.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Do you know when the malolactic finished?

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
It’s a child’s birth year for me, so I’d already dabbled in 2020 futures, but my takeaways from WK’s notes and after also comparing to JL’s were the following:

  • I’m glad I bought some Canon early on; wish I had gone for more (also more Rauzan-Segla).
  • Value-wise (for 2020 releases, at present prices, and from my underinformed view), Rauzan-Segla, Troplong Mondot seem like pretty solid purchases at just over $100 USD; Giscours, Beau-Sejour Becot, Domaine de Chevalier, Durfort Vivens, Lagrange under ~$70 USD.
  • I need to be better about working out so I can actually enjoy these in my lifetime. They are birth year wines, but my kid has to earn any that go his way, ultimately .

I don’t have time to go through the whole report, but ~$200 delivered seemed OK for (i) 100 pointer from William Kelley, (ii) bottles were reviewed with more time in the bottle and (iii) both are 13.x% alcohol wines in a time of rising alcohols. These are the wines I want to drink and it doesn’t seem they will be getting any cheaper. I enjoyed 2019 Montrose but regretted how deep I purchased given it is a lot higher alcohol (14.4% vs. 13.7%)… 2019 was a bit cheaper though and can still be found for a bit less than the 2020 (I wonder if that is partially reflective of it’s higher alcohol content).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: In Bordeaux malolactic is generally done before the end of the year of the vintage.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: There was one vintage and I can’t remember which, where malo was very late, and the wines showed less well than usual There was some chat about many 2020s initially being awkward, and wondered if there was a late malo as well.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: That would be showing badly during en primeur, which is now in April but which was formerly in March after the vintage - so a late malolactic could indeed render the wines very hard to taste. Two and a half years later I don’t think it makes much difference, as we are only talking about a month or two of difference at most in Bordeaux.

posts.name: Justin S ! n g h
posts.cooked:
One thing William’s excellent report on 2020 Bordeaux got me thinking about is the relative value to Burgundy. I REALLY don’t want to turn this into a Burgundy vs. Bordeaux thread, but it came up in the context of deciding how many bottles of 2020 Rauzan Segla to buy. For ~$100 you can presumably have one of the top wines from the vintage according to WK’s ratings. I was also looking at buying some 2020 Bouchard Volnay Cuvee Ancienne for ~ $90 / bottle. WK rated that wine quite well too, but it is not among the top wines of the vintage. A+ vs. A- if you will, relative to what is possible in each respective region.

Hopefully, this is a fair way to interpet the relative scores, since I do not think the wines can be compared directly against each other.

As an aside @William_Kelley , I find myself trying to go to williamkelley.com instead of robertparker.com to check scores!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

Perhaps. However that 52 Lafite mag last weekend was a spiritual experience for me and renewed my belief in BDX’s ability to rock my world with the best of Burg. I just think my scale is probably a bit skewed from the BDX lover who finds pleasure from average and above average wines.

A bit skewed, or a bit jaded!

Put me in that latter camp of finding great pleasure from what you probably consider to be simple wines. I’ve had many of those revelatory wines you exalt - heck 20+ years of Bern’s, which is just 90 miles away alone takes me to the promise land. From classic Bordeaux from every decade from 1918 and up. And many others with friends, family, and occasionally alone, when I have the whim to pop a unicorn or a special or even so-called 100 point wine. But if that is how I measure my appreciation of wine, I personally would feel that I missed the mark. I have as much reverence for that 1983 Cantemerle, 1975 Lanessan, 1990 La Louviere, 1982 Sociando, and so many many other fine Cru Bordeaux and Haut Medocs that, to me, really capture the essence of this most noble wine region. It’s the appreciation of popping mature, value-oriented, quality wines that you can pop whenever and generally pair well with so many dishes. I will acknowledge I define myself more as a drinker than a collector.

You could have just read Jeff’s tasting note and known you wouldn’t have liked it: “Concentrated, full-bodied, deep and opulently textured, there is a beautiful sense of elegance and balance here. There is richness, power but also symmetry. The purity in the fruit, along with its freshness stand out as one of the keynotes of its character.”

Here’s Jeff on the 2020: “The wine is rich, full-bodied, concentrated, seductive and packed from start to finish with layers of crushed rocks, licorice, blackberries, currants and cocoa, ending with light touches of chalk, along with sweet red and black pit fruits that build as they linger.”

Fairly consistent.

To be fair, there his note on the 2010: “Palate coating richness, luscious black plums, cherries, dark chocolate, licorice, espresso and hints of blue fruit get all over your mouth, palate, teeth and gums. The wine is pure velvet in texture with layers of opulent fruits and a finish that sticks.” [N.B. I love this note.]

So it seems they dialed it back from “palate-coating richness,” “luscious” and “pure velvet” with “opulent fruits” to rich, concentrated and full-bodied.

posts.name: Heiko Sklenar
posts.cooked:
Here is William’s bottle rating on Troplong Mondot (98+).

The 2020 Troplong Mondot may prove to be even better than the brilliant 2019, and it is one of the Right Bank’s finest wines in this vintage. Unwinding in the glass with deep aromas of mulberries, blackberries and red cherries mingled with hints of rose petal, licorice and exotic spices, it’s medium to full-bodied, deep and layered, with a satiny attack that segues into a vibrant, polished mid-palate, concluding with a long, resonant finish.

14.5% alcohol. Quite a lot, but not too much either.

posts.name: Charles Fuss
posts.cooked: Im in north jerz and took delivery on some 2019 Lynch-Bages mags … id like to trade one in person for a 2020. Let me know if interested

@William_Kelley I read your report on the 2020 vintage and I have a technical/terminology question.
In the Lafite note, you state that it’s relatively high pH means ‘it will never shut down’. I’m assuming this is a reference to a ‘dumb phase’? How does a high pH contribute to preventing that? I thought lower pH meant more stable ageing and that higher pH meant a less stable and ageworthy wine?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
While sometimes why red wines shut down can be rather mysterious, the combination most likely to shut down in bottle is low pH, high levels of tannin, and a high redox potential. High pH red wines shut down less as higher pH makes tannin less perceptible / seem suppler.

It is true that higher pH wines are less microbially stable, so they require more vigilance during maturation and at bottling. But that is a very different subject from whether or not they shut down in bottle.

Hope that clarifies matters!
